changing the interpretation of existing tax laws. Certain of these proposals and interpretations, if
implemented or upheld, could have the effect of diminishing the value of obligations of states and
their political subdivisions, such as the Series 2018 Bonds, by eliminating or changing the tax-
exempt status of interest on certain of such bonds. Whether any of such proposals will ultimately
become or be upheld as law, and if so, the effect such proposals could have upon the value of
bonds such as the Series 2018 Bonds, cannot be predicted. However, it is possible that any such
law or interpretation could have a material and adverse effect upon the availability of a liquid
secondary market and/or the value of the Series 2018 Bonds. See also "TAX MATTERS."

State Tax Reform

        It is impossible to predict what new proposals may be presented regarding ad valorem tax
reform and/or special districts during upcoming legislative sessions, whether such new proposals
or any previous proposals regarding the same will be adopted by the Florida Senate and House of
Representatives and signed by the Governor, and, if adopted, the form thereof. On October 31,
2014, the Auditor General of the State released a 31-page report which requests legislative action
to establish parameters on the amount of bonds a community development district may issue and
provide additional oversight for special district bonds. This report renews requests made by the
Auditor General in 2011 that led to the Governor of the State issuing an Executive Order on
January 11, 2012 (the "Executive Order") directing the Office of Policy and Budget in the
Executive Office of the Governor ("OPB") to examine the role of special districts in the State. As
of the date hereof, the OPB has not made any recommendations pursuant to the Executive Order
nor has the Florida legislature passed any related legislation. It is impossible to predict with
certainty the impact that any existing or future legislation will or may have on the security for the
Series 2018 Bonds. It should be noted that Section 10(p) of the Act provides in pertinent part that
"The state pledges to the holders of any bonds issued under this act that it will not limit or alter the
rights of the district … to levy and collect the … assessments … and to fulfill the terms of any
agreement made with the holders of such bonds … and that it will not in any way impair the rights
or remedies of such holders."

Insufficient Resources or Other Factors causing Failure to Complete the Series 2018 Project
or the Development of, and Construction of Homes within, the Series 2018 Assessment Areas

        In the event the District does not have sufficient moneys on hand to complete the Series
2018 Project, there can be no assurance that the District will be able to raise through the issuance
of bonds, or otherwise, the moneys necessary to complete the Series 2018 Project. Further,
pursuant to the Series 2018 Indenture, the District will covenant not to issue any other Bonds or
other debt obligations secured by any of the Series 2018 Special Assessments. Such covenant
shall not prohibit the District from issuing refunding bonds. In addition, the District will covenant


                                                  47

not to issue any other Bonds or other debt obligations secured by special assessments levied on
assessable lands within Assessment Area 2B or Assessment Area 4, which are also encumbered
by the Assessment Area 2B Assessments or Assessment Area 4 Assessments, respectively, for any
capital project, unless the Assessment Area 2B Assessments or Assessment Area 4 Assessments,
as applicable have been Substantially Absorbed. However, the Assessment Area 3A Indenture
allows the District to issue certain limited amounts of additional Bonds or other debt obligations
secured by special assessments levied on assessable lands within Assessment Area 3A prior to
Substantial Absorption. The District is permitted to issue Bonds or other debt obligations on lands
within the District for any capital project where no Series 2018 Special Assessments are levied.
See "SECURITY FOR AND SOURCE OF PAYMENT OF THE SERIES 2018
BONDS - Additional Bonds" for more information.

        The costs of the Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project
are expected to be sufficient to develop finished lots in Assessment Area 2B. The remaining
portions of the Series 2018 Master Project allocable to Assessment Area 3A and Assessment Area
4 do not include the costs of developing finished lots in Assessment Area 3A or Assessment Area
4. The costs to develop finished lots in the Series 2018 Assessment Areas exceed the costs of the
Series 2018 Project. The Developer will agree to fund or cause to be funded the completion of the
Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project and will enter into two
Completion Agreements with the District as evidence thereof. There can be no assurance that the
Developer will have sufficient resources to do so. Such obligation of the Developer is an
unsecured obligation. The Developer is a single purpose investment entity created for the purpose
of developing the Babcock Ranch Community. The Developer's sole asset is the land it owns in
the Babcock Ranch Community. The Developer is not obligated to develop finished lots or
complete any neighborhood infrastructure improvements for Assessment Area 3A or Assessment
Area 4. See "THE DEVELOPMENT" and "THE DEVELOPER" herein.

Payment of Series 2018 Special Assessments after Bank Foreclosure

        In the event a bank forecloses on property because of a default on the mortgage on any
lands within the Series 2018 Assessment Areas, and then the bank itself fails, the Federal Deposit
Insurance Corporation (the "FDIC"), as receiver, will then become the fee owner of such property.
In such event, the FDIC will not, pursuant to its own rules and regulations, likely be liable to pay
the Series 2018 Special Assessments levied on such property. In addition, the District would
require the consent of the FDIC prior to commencing a foreclosure action.

                                                                                   50

                                         THE DISTRICT

General Information

        The District is an independent special district duly established and existing pursuant to
Chapter 2007-306, Laws of Florida, effective on June 27, 2007, as amended by Chapter 2016-257,
Laws of Florida, effective on March 25, 2016 (collectively, the "Act"), and is a public body
corporate and politic, an independent, limited, special purpose local government, and independent
special district under Sections 189.031 and 189.012(3), Florida Statutes. The boundaries of the
District contain approximately 13,630 acres of land in Charlotte County and an additional
approximately 4,157 acres located in Lee County (collectively, the "District Lands"). The District
Lands are being developed as part of a large-scale, mixed-use development known as "Babcock
Ranch" (referred to herein as the "Babcock Ranch Community"). See "THE DEVELOPMENT"
for more information.

Legal Powers and Authority

         The District is an independent unit of local government created pursuant to, and established
in accordance with, the Act. The Act authorizes the planning, constructing, maintaining, operating,
financing and improving the provision of systems, facilities and services necessary to meet the
infrastructure needs of the Babcock Ranch Community. The Act provides legal authority for the
District to issue bonds pursuant to its general powers. The District is classified as an independent
district under Chapter 189, Florida Statutes.

        The Act gives the District's Governing Board the authority to, among other things: (i) plan,
establish, acquire, construct or reconstruct, enlarge or extend, equip, operate, finance, fund and
maintain improvements, systems, facilities, services, works, projects and infrastructure, including
without limitation any obligations pursuant to a development order, and further including without
limitation: (a) water management and control for lands within the District and connection any of
such facilities with roads and bridges; (b) water supply, sewer and waste-water management,
reclamation and reuse systems or any combination thereof, irrigation systems, and construction
and operation of connecting intercepting or outlet sewers and sewer mains and pipes and water
mains, conduits, or pipelines in, along, and under any street, alley, highway, or other public place
or ways, and disposal of any effluent, residue, or other byproducts of such system or sewer system;
(c) transportation and transportation-related improvements, equal to or exceeding the
specifications of the county in which such improvements are located, including without limitation
highways, streets, roads, alleys, trails, pathways, sidewalks, parkways, bicycle lanes, jogging
paths, interchanges, bridges, thoroughfares of all kinds, landscaping and hardscaping, irrigation,
storm drains, street lighting, traffic signals, signage, parking facilities and undergrounding of
cable, fiber or wire lines; (d) parks and facilities for indoor and outdoor recreational, cultural,
educational and library uses; (v) fire prevention and control; (e) emergency medical and rescue
response services; (f) school buildings and related structures for use in the educational system
when authorized by the affected school board; and (g) any facilities or improvements that may
otherwise be provided for by any county or municipality, including but not limited to libraries,
annexes, substations and other buildings to house public officials, staff and employees; (ii) borrow
money and issue bonds of the District; (iii) determine, order, levy, impose, collect and enforce
assessments pursuant to the Act and the general laws of the State; and (iv) exercise all of the


                                                 51

powers necessary, convenient, incidental or proper in connection with any of the powers
authorized in the Act.

        The Act does not empower the District to adopt and enforce any land use plans or zoning
ordinances and the Act does not empower the District to grant building permits; these functions
are to be performed by general purpose local governments having jurisdiction over the lands within
the District.

       The Act exempts all property owned by the District from levy and sale by virtue of an
execution and from judgment liens, but does not limit the right of any bondowners to pursue any
remedy for enforcement of any lien or pledge given by the District in connection with its bonds,
including the Series 2018 Bonds.

Governing Board

         The governing body of the District is its Governing Board (the "Board"), which is
composed of five voting members (the "Members"). The Act provides that, at the initial meeting
of the landowners, the landowners would elect the Members, with the two Members receiving the
highest number of votes being elected to serve terms expiring November 30, 2010 and the
remaining Members being elected to serve terms expiring November 30, 2008. Thereafter,
elections are to be held every two years in November, with Members elected in or after November
2008 serving four-year terms. The Act provides for the transition of the Board to control by
qualified electors, beginning with the first general election following a trigger of the qualified
elector population thresholds in the Act, as follows: (i) once 4,600 qualified electors reside in the
District, one Member shall be elected by the qualified electors and four Members shall be elected
by the landowners; (ii) once 8,900 qualified electors reside in the District, two Members shall be
elected by the qualified electors and three Members shall be elected by the landowners; (iii) once
22,000 qualified electors reside in the District, three Members shall be elected by the qualified
electors and two Members shall be elected by the landowners; (iv) once 24,000 qualified electors
reside in the District, four Members shall be elected by the qualified electors and one Member
shall be elected by the landowners; and (v) once 25,000 qualified electors reside in the District, all
Members shall be elected by the qualified electors. The Board may not exercise its ad valorem
taxing power until such time as all members of the Board are elected by qualified electors. At
elections in which landowners are electing Members, each landowner shall be entitled to cast one
vote per acre of land owned within the District for each person to be elected, with fractions of an
acre being treated as one acre. A "qualified elector" under the Act is any person at least 18 years
of age or older, who is a citizen of the United States, a legal resident of the State and the District,
and who registers to vote with the Supervisor of Elections in Charlotte or Lee County. The Board
must determine the number of qualified electors in the District on or before June 1 of each year,
as of the immediately preceding April 15, and the determination must be made at a properly
noticed meeting of the Board. If there is a vacancy on the Board, the remaining Board members
are to fill such vacancy by appointment for the unexpired term.

       The Act provides that it shall not be an impermissible conflict of interest under Florida law
governing public officials for a Member to be a stockholder, officer or employee of a landowner.




                                                  52

        The current members of the Board and the expiration of the term of each member are set
forth below:

              Name                                 Title                   Term Expires
      Gary Nelson*                            Chairperson                November 30, 2022

      Elizabeth Andres*                       Assistant Secretary        November 30, 2020
      Gail Maltese*                           Assistant Secretary        November 30, 2020
___________________
 *
   Employee of, or affiliated with, the Developer.

        A majority of the members of the Board constitutes a quorum for the purposes of
conducting its business and exercising its powers and for all other purposes. Action taken by the
District shall be upon a vote of a majority of the Members present unless general law or a rule of
the District requires a greater number. All meetings of the Board are open to the public under
Florida's open meeting or "Sunshine" law.

The District Manager and Other Consultants

        The chief administrative official of the District is the District Manager (as hereinafter
defined). The Act provides that a district manager has charge and supervision of the works of the
District and is responsible for preserving and maintaining any improvement or facility constructed
or erected pursuant to the provisions of the Act, for maintaining and operating the equipment
owned by the District, and for performing such other duties as may be prescribed by the Board.

        The District has retained Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC, Boca Raton, Florida, to
serve as its district manager ("District Manager"). The District Manager's office is located at 2300
Glades Road, Suite #410W, Boca Raton, Florida, 33431.

        The Act further authorizes the Board to hire such employees and agents as it deems
necessary. Thus, the District has employed the services of Greenberg Traurig, P.A., Orlando,
Florida, as Bond Counsel and Disclosure Counsel; Kimley-Horn and Associates, Inc., Fort Myers,
Florida, as District Engineer; and Hopping Green & Sams, P.A., Tallahassee, Florida, as District
Counsel. The Board has also retained the District Manager to serve as Methodology Consultant
and to prepare the Assessment Methodology for the Series 2018 Bonds. The Board also intends
to retain Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC, as Dissemination Agent for the Series 2018 Bonds.

Outstanding Indebtedness

       The District has previously issued its $19,955,000 Special Assessment Revenue Bonds,
Series 2015 (the "Assessment Area 1 Bonds") under and pursuant to the Master Indenture, as
supplemented by that certain First Supplemental Trust Indenture dated as of December 1, 2015,
by and between the District and the Trustee, of which $19,605,000 in principal amount is currently
outstanding.

       The Assessment Area 1 Bonds are secured by special assessments levied on a separate
assessment area and are not on parity with the Series 2018 Bonds. The Assessment Area 1 Bonds


                                                     53

are secured by assessments imposed, levied and collected by the District on the approximately 596
gross acres of land being developed as 661 single-family residential lots of varying sizes, 108
townhomes, 330 condominium units along with approximately 64,350 square feet of commercial
and retail space. See "THE DEVELOPMENT – Update to Assessment Area 1 and Assessment
Area 2A" herein.

       The District has previously issued its $2,075,000 Special Assessment Revenue Bonds,
Series 2018 (the "Assessment Area 2A Bonds") under and pursuant to the Master Indenture, as
supplemented by that certain Second Supplemental Trust Indenture dated as of April 1, 2018, by
and between the District and the Trustee, of which $2,075,000, in principal amount is currently
outstanding.

       The Assessment Area 2A Bonds are secured by special assessments levied on a separate
assessment area and not on parity with the Series 2018 Bonds. The Assessment Area 2A Bonds
are secured by assessments imposed, levied and collected by the District on the approximately
59.96 gross acres of land planned and platted for 176 residential lots. See "THE
DEVELOPMENT - Update to Assessment Area 1 and Assessment Area 2A" herein.

(the "District Engineer"), sets forth certain public infrastructure improvements to be constructed
to support the development of the next phases of development which includes the approximately
(i) 85.8 gross acres of development planned for 243 residential units in Assessment Area 2B, (ii)
400.1 gross acres of development planned for 1,053 residential units in Assessment Area 3A, and
(iii) 74.1 gross acres of development planned for 166 residential units in Assessment Area 4
(collectively, the "Series 2018 Project"). The Series 2018 Project has two components: (i) the
"Series 2018 Master Project" which provides the master infrastructure improvements necessary to
convey unfinished lots to the Builders (as defined herein) in each of the Series 2018 Assessment
Areas; and (ii) the "Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project" which provides the neighborhood
infrastructure improvements necessary to convey finished lots to Lennar Homes in Assessment
Area 2B. See "THE DEVELOPMENT" for more information. In the Engineer's Report, the
District Engineer estimates the total cost to complete the Series 2018 Project to be approximately
$22,792,000, which consists of the following.

Series 2018 Master Project

        The Series 2018 Master Project commenced in the fourth quarter of 2018, will be
constructed in sub-phases and is expected to be substantially complete in the fourth quarter of
2021. The Series 2018 Master Project is comprised of master infrastructure improvements across
all of the Series 2018 Assessment Areas that benefit an aggregate of approximately 1,430
residential units over approximately 560 gross acres of residential and amenity development, open
space, lakes, preserves and right-of-ways. Roadways and utilities will be constructed to access the
Series 2018 Assessment Areas which include streetscape, landscape and hardscape.

Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project

        Assessment Area 2B includes both the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project as well as a
portion of Series 2018 Master Project. The Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project is comprised of
internal improvements as located in, and benefitting only the lands within Assessment Area 2B as
detailed in the table above. The Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project commenced in the fourth
quarter of 2018 and is expected to be substantially complete in the first quarter of 2020. The


                                                         55

District Engineer has indicated that certain permits necessary to construct the Series 2018 Projects
have been obtained or are expected to be obtained in the ordinary course of completion of the
Series 2018 Projects.       In addition to the Engineer's Report, please refer to "THE
DEVELOPMENT - Development Approvals and Permits" for a more detailed description of the
entitlement and permitting status of the Development.

        Developer equity and net proceeds of the Series 2018 Bonds are expected to fund the Costs
of the Series 2018 Project, including reimbursing a portion of the costs borne by the Developer.
The Developer will enter into two completion agreements, one with respect to the Series 2018
Master Project and the other with respect to the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project to fund or
cause to be funded the completion of each component of the Series 2018 Project to the extent that
net proceeds of the Series 2018 Bonds are not sufficient to pay such cost. See "BONDOWNERS'
RISKS - Insufficient Resources or Other Factors causing Failure to Complete the Series 2018
Project or the Development of, and Construction of Homes" herein.


     ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY AND THE ALLOCATION OF SERIES 2018
                         ASSESSMENTS

General

       The Babcock Ranch Community Independent Special District Master Special Assessment
Methodology Report For Phase 3 dated December 5, 2018, as supplemented by the Babcock Ranch
Community Independent Special District Supplemental Special Assessment Methodology Report
for Phase 3 dated December 13, 2018 (collectively, the "Assessment Methodology") which
describes the methodology for the allocation of the Assessment Area 2B Assessments to lands
within Assessment Area 2B, the Assessment Area 3A Assessments to lands within Assessment
Area 3A, and the Assessment Area 4 Assessments to lands within Assessment Area 4, has been
prepared by Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC, Boca Raton, Florida (the "Methodology
Consultant"). See "EXPERTS" herein for more information. The Assessment Methodology is
included as APPENDIX D attached hereto. Once the final terms of the Series 2018 Bonds are
determined, the Assessment Methodology will be further supplemented to reflect such final terms.
Once levied and imposed, each of the Series 2018 Assessments are a first lien on the respective
portion of assessable land in the respective Series 2018 Assessment Area until paid or barred by
operation of law, co-equal with other taxes and assessments levied by the District and other units
of government. See "ENFORCEMENT OF ASSESSMENT COLLECTIONS" herein.

approved millages levied for general obligation bonds, as to which no limit applies, Charlotte
County and the School District of Charlotte County, Florida, may each levy ad valorem taxes upon
the land in the Series 2018 Assessment Areas up to 10 mills each. The millage rate applicable to
property located within the Charlotte Development in tax year 2017 was approximately 16.03160
mills. The District has no control over the level of ad valorem taxes and/or special assessments
levied by other taxing authorities. It is possible that in future years taxes levied by these other
entities could be substantially higher than in the current year.                       See "THE

Developer will make a representation and warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of such
information to the extent supplied by it.




                                                                      59

        The following information is provided by the Developer as a means for the prospective
bondholders to understand the anticipated development plan and risks associated with the
Development. The Developer's obligations to pay the Series 2018 Special Assessments are no
greater than the obligation of any other landowner or subsequent landowner within the Assessment
Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A and Assessment Area 4 of the District. The Developer is not a
guarantor of payment as to any land within the District and the recourse for the Developer's failure
to pay is limited to its ownership interests in the land.


                                    THE DEVELOPMENT

General

        The District Lands are being developed as part of a large-scale, mixed-use development
known as "Babcock Ranch" (referred to herein as the "Babcock Ranch Community"). The
aggregate 17,787 acres of land in the Babcock Ranch Community are located in both Charlotte
and Lee Counties and are planned for approximately 19,500 residential units and 3,000,000 square
feet of commercial space. The portion of the District Lands within Charlotte County is referred to
herein as the "Charlotte Development" or the "Development"). The Charlotte Development
contains approximately 13,630 acres and constitutes the Master DRI (as defined herein). Under
the Master DRI, the Charlotte Development is authorized for 17,870 residential units and
5,910,000 square feet of commercial and industrial space, as well as 177 hospital beds, 418 assisted
living units, 54 golf holes and ancillary facilities such as educational and research facilities,
libraries, places of worship, parks, and utility infrastructure. The portion of the District Lands
within Lee County is referred to herein as the "Lee Development." The Lee Development contains
approximately 4,157 acres and is authorized for 1,630 residential units, 600 hotel rooms and
1,170,000 square feet of commercial office and retail uses.

         Babcock Ranch Community is being developed as an eco-centric new town embedded in
nature that is energy conscious and technologically advanced. The community is host to solar
energy via a 74.5 Megawatt facility built and operated by Florida Power & Light ("FP&L") and
will be a master-planned "smart city" with emphasis on the environment, health and wellness,
education, energy, technology, transportation and storm safety. Water, wastewater and sewer
services will be provided by Babcock Ranch Water Utilities, a department of the District, through
an agreement with MSKP Town and Country Utility, LLC, an affiliate of the Developer. See
" - Utilities and the Utility System" below for more information. The Babcock Ranch Community
will be surrounded by a 73,000-acre permanent nature preserve and will contain approximately
9,000 acres of natural greenways, parks and lakes, as well as over 50 miles of trails.

       The development and build-out of Assessment Area 1 and Assessment Area 2A (each as
defined herein) have commenced and are on-going. See "THE DISTRICT - Outstanding
Indebtedness" herein and "– Update to Assessment Area 1 and Assessment Area 2A" below. The
next phases of development are the approximately: (i) 85.8 gross acres of development planned
for 243 residential units in Assessment Area 2B, (ii) 400.1 gross acres of development planned for
1,053 residential units in Assessment Area 3A, and (iii) 74.1 gross acres of development planned
for 166 residential units in Assessment Area 4.




                                                60

       Babcock Property Holdings, L.L.C., a Delaware limited liability company (the
"Developer"), is the master developer of the Development. The Developer has entered into Builder
Contracts to sell all of the lots in the Series 2018 Assessment Areas. See " - Builder Contracts and
Builders" below.

Assessment Area 2B

        The Developer has entered into a Lot Purchase Agreement dated April 16, 2018, as
amended (the "Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract") with Lennar Homes, LLC, a Florida
limited liability company ("Lennar Homes"), for the sale of developed lots in Assessment Area
2B. Lennar Homes will construct and market homes for sale and plans to continue upon the
success they have had where they have averaged twelve sales per month in Assessment Area 2A.
See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below.

Assessment Area 3A

        The Developer has entered into a Lot Purchase Agreement dated June 12, 2018, as
amended (the "Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract") with Lennar Homes, for the bulk sale of
undeveloped land within Assessment Area 3A. Lennar Homes will acquire the parcel upon the
terms set forth in the Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract and will install the onsite infrastructure
and sell homes to homebuyers. Additionally, Lennar Homes plans to construct a golf course and
market Assessment Area 3A as "Babcock National," a bundled golf community with a variety of
product types, similar to what they have done throughout the South West Florida market for
decades. It is expected that the closing with Lennar Homes under the Assessment Area 3A Builder
Contract is expected to take place sometime between December 28, 2018 and mid-March 2019.
See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below.

Assessment Area 4

        The Developer has also entered into a Lot Purchase Agreement dated September 27, 2018,
as amended (the "Assessment Area 4 Builder Contract" and, together with the Assessment Area
2B Builder Contract and the Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract, the "Builder Contracts") with
Pulte Home Company, LLC, a Michigan limited liability company ("Pulte" and, together with
Lennar Homes, the "Builders"), a builder of residential homes, for the bulk sale of undeveloped
land within Assessment Area 4. See " – Builder Contracts and Builders" below and "THE
DEVELOPER" herein for more information regarding the Builder Contracts, Lennar Homes, Pulte
and the Developer. Pulte will acquire the parcel and install the onsite infrastructure and construct
and market homes for sale to homebuyers. Pulte is already a builder in Babcock Ranch
Community and plans to continue upon the success they have had where they have averaged six
sales per month during calendar year 2018. See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below.

Update to Assessment Area 1 and Assessment Area 2A

       Assessment Area 1. The District issued its Assessment Area 1 Bonds to finance certain
master and onsite infrastructure improvements. Upon full platting, the Assessment Area 1 Bonds
are expected to be secured by 1,099 lots. To date, $33,000,000 has been spent on developing the
land within Assessment Area 1 securing the Assessment Area 1 Bonds. To date, approximately
675 lots have been developed and platted. Additionally, FPL finished construction on the 74.5
Megawatt solar power facility and recently announced plans for a battery storage program that will
allow excess power created during the day to be stored in batteries that can be used by the
community at night when solar power is not being generated.

Land Acquisition and Financing Plan

       The Developer acquired the original roughly 17,800 acres of land in the Babcock Ranch
Community on July 31, 2006. To date, the Developer has invested over $260,000,000 in
connection with its acquisition and development of the Babcock Ranch Community. In addition,
the Developer has spent approximately $33,800,000 towards the $35,000,000 of costs required for



                                              65

constructing the amenities, commercial and retail portions of Babcock Ranch Community. See
" – Amenities; Commercial/Retail Development" below. None of the Developer's lands in the
Development are subject to a mortgage.

        The Developer expects the costs of the Series 2018 Master Project to be approximately
$19,784,000, and the costs of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project to be approximately
$1,520,000. Completion of the Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
Project will result in (i) the completion of the master infrastructure necessary of the three Series
2018 Assessment Areas, and (ii) the completion of the neighborhood infrastructure necessary for
developing finished lots in Assessment Area 2B.

        A portion of the proceeds of the Series 2018 Bonds will fund approximately $13,000,000
of the Series 2018 Master Project costs and the Developer will fund the remaining Series 2018
Master Project costs with equity. Likewise proceeds of the Assessment Area 2B Bonds will fund
approximately $1,000,000 of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project costs, with the remaining
costs being funded with Developer equity. Developer equity and the net proceeds of the Series
2018 Bonds are expected to fund the entirety of the Series 2018 Project costs. The remaining
parcel infrastructure necessary to develop Assessment Area 3A and Assessment Area 4 will be
constructed by Lennar Homes and Pulte, respectively. The Developer currently expects that,
excluding costs relating to the golf course planned as part of Assessment Area 3A, the costs for
developing lots in Assessment Areas 3A and 4 could range between $35,000 to $50,000 per lot
inclusive of common area and amenity improvements depending upon the ultimate development
plan. See "THE CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM AND THE SERIES 2018 PROJECT"
herein and "APPENDIX C - ENGINEER'S REPORT" attached hereto for more information
regarding the Series 2018 Project.

        The Developer will enter into two completion agreements, one with respect to the Series
2018 Master Project and the other with respect to the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project to
fund or cause to be funded the completion of each component of the Series 2018 Project to the
extent that net proceeds of the Series 2018 Bonds are not sufficient to pay such cost. See
"BONDOWNERS' RISKS - Insufficient Resources or Other Factors causing Failure to Complete
the Series 2018 Project or the Development of, and Construction of Homes" herein.

Development Plan and Status

        Series 2018 Master Project. The Series 2018 Master Project commenced in the fourth
quarter of 2018, will be constructed in sub-phases and is expected to be substantially complete in
the fourth quarter of 2021. The Series 2018 Master Project is comprised of master infrastructure
improvements across all of the Series 2018 Assessment Areas that benefit an aggregate of
approximately 1,430 residential units over approximately 560 gross acres of residential and
amenity development, open space, lakes, preserves and right-of-ways. Roadways and utilities will
be constructed to access the Series 2018 Assessment Areas which include streetscape, landscape
and hardscape.




                                                66

       The development plan and status of the Series 2018 Assessment Areas are set forth below:

        Assessment Area 2B. The development of Assessment Area 2B commenced in the fourth
quarter of 2018, will be completed in sub-phases, and is expected to be substantially complete in
the fourth quarter of 2020. Lennar Homes will commence takedowns of finished lots in the
second quarter of 2019. See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below.

       Assessment Area 3A. The development of Assessment Area 3A will commence in the first
quarter of 2019, with substantial completion of the portion of the Series 2018 Master Project
allocable to Assessment Area 3A expected to occur in the fourth quarter of 2021. The Series
2018 Master Project will provide roadway and utility access to Assessment Area 3A. Lennar
Homes will install the onsite infrastructure. Lennar Homes is expected to issue additional bonds
secured by Assessments on Assessment Area 3A, likely in multiple phases, to finance a portion of
the onsite infrastructure. See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below and "SECURITY FOR
AND SOURCE OF PAYMENT OF THE SERIES 2018 BONDS – Additional Bonds" herein for
more information regarding the limitations on additional Bonds secured by Assessments levied on
assessable land in Assessment Area 3A.

        Assessment Area 4. The development of Assessment Area 4 will commence in the fourth
quarter of 2019, with the substantial completion of the portion of the Series 2018 Master Project
allocable to Assessment Area 4 expected to occur in the third quarter of 2020. The Series 2018
Master Project will provide roadway and utility access to Assessment Area 4. Pulte will install
the onsite infrastructure. See " - Builder Contracts and Builders" below.

Builder Contracts and Builders

         Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract. The Developer and Lennar Homes entered into that
certain Babcock Ranch Lot Purchase Agreement dated April 16, 2018, as amended by the First
Amendment to Babcock Ranch Lot Purchase Agreement dated June 6, 2018 (collectively, the
"Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract"), whereby the Developer will sell 67 34-foot wide lots,
78 52-foot wide lots, and 65 62-foot wide lots to Lennar Homes, and Lennar Homes will construct,
market and sell homes on the lots. The Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract provides for an
initial takedown of 35 lots after the satisfaction of certain conditions precedent (the "First Closing")
with the remaining lots being taken down in 25-lot increments over a 21-month period following
the First Closing. Base lot purchase prices under the Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract are
(i) $19,600 for each 34' lot, (ii) $35,000 for each 52' lot, and (iii) $37,500 for each 62' lot. In
addition to the base lot purchase price for each lot it purchases from the Developer, Lennar Homes
will pay additional deferred compensation in an amount equal to 18% of the difference between
the adjusted finished home sale price less the base lot purchase price (the adjusted finished home
sale price being the total home price paid by Lennar Homes’ buyer, including options and extras
other than the swimming pool package, less broker’s commission, furniture and marketing fees),
as well as a 3% base purchase price escalator, commencing two years after the First Closing.
Lennar Homes has posted a $364,500 letter of credit as a deposit under the Assessment Area 2B
Builder Contract. The letter of credit secures Lennar Homes' performance of its obligations under
the Assessment Area 2B Builder Contract, and shall be reduced by $1,500 upon each lot closing.
See " - Residential Product Offerings" below.



                                                  67

         Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract. The Developer and Lennar Homes, entered into
that certain Babcock Ranch Purchase Agreement (Village 3 Golf Development), dated June 12,
2018, as amended by the First Amendment to Babcock Ranch Purchase Agreement (Village 3 Golf
Development) dated August 2, 2018 (collectively, the "Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract"),
whereby the Developer will sell a parcel of land consisting of approximately 454.68 acres with the
intention that Lennar Homes will develop and construct thereon a residential golf course bundled
community consisting of approximately 1,053 residential units to be constructed by Lennar Homes
including single-family residences, condominium units, an 18-hole golf course and a community
amenity center. The closing of title to the property within Assessment Area 3A is expected to take
place on the later of December 31, 2018, or twenty days after Lennar Homes' receipt of both final,
non-appealable preliminary site plan approval from Charlotte County and South Florida Water
Management District approval. Lennar Homes has agreed to purchase the property within
Assessment Area 3A for a base price of $10,000,000. In addition to the base purchase price,
Lennar Homes will pay, as additional deferred compensation for Assessment Area 3A a true-up
payment computed as follows: (i) for the first 50% of the homes sold by Lennar Homes within
Assessment Area 3A, an amount equal to 9% of the net home price and thereafter for the remaining
homes an amount equal to 10% of the net home price, (ii) less an amount equal to the base purchase
price divided by the total number of homes planned for Assessment Area 3A. The net home price
shall be calculated as the total home price as set forth on the closing statement by the sale by
Lennar Homes to a third-party homebuyer of a home inclusive of all options and extras, less
broker's commissions paid by Lennar Homes, the marketing fee and the price of furniture, if
applicable. Pursuant to the Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract, Lennar Homes is responsible
for its own home marketing and sales operation. Lennar Homes has posted a $2,400,000 letter of
credit as a deposit under the Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract. The letter of credit secures
Lennar Homes' performance of its obligations under the Assessment Area 3A Builder Contract;
however, it is not credited against the base purchase price at the closing but rather is held as
security for Lennar’s initial true-up payments. It will be reduced on a quarterly basis based upon
the number of home closings to third-party purchasers occurring during the previous quarter, such
reduction being equal to $10,000 for each home (regardless of the amount of the true-up payment
paid to Lennar Homes as a result of such closing).

        Assessment Area 4 Builder Contract. The Developer and Pulte entered into that certain
Babcock Ranch Lot Purchase Agreement (Babcock Ranch Earthsource Parcel) dated September
27, 2018, as amended by the First Amendment to Babcock Ranch Lot Puchase Agreement dated
December 6, 2018, as further amended by the Second Amendment to Babcock Ranch Lot
Purchaser Agreement dated December 19, 2018 (collectively, the "Assessment Area 4 Builder
Contract"), whereby the Developer will sell a parcel of land consisting of approximately 74.33
acres with the intention that Pulte will develop and construct thereon a residential community
consisting of approximately 166 single-family detached homes together with a community amenity
center. Pulte shall pay at the closing a base purchase price equal to the sum of $15,000 multiplied
by the number of lots in Pulte's project as determined by the plat. Pursuant to the Assessment Area
4 Builder Contract the expected closing is the first business day that is 30 days after the later of (i)
Pulte's receipt of the builder approvals; (ii) Developer's completion of certain pre-closing
developer work, and (iii) Developer's recordation of the plat. In addition to the base price, Pulte
will pay, as additional deferred compensation for the 52' and 66' lots (not including the 42' lots) a
true-up payment computed as follows: (a) for the first 50% of the homes in Assessment Area 4
that are sold, the true-up payments shall be equal to 9% of the adjusted sales price less the pro-


                                                  68

rated base purchase price; (b) for the remainder of the homes in Assessment Area 4 that are sold,
the true-up payment shall be equal to 10% of the adjusted sales price less the pro-rated base
purchase price. The adjusted sales price shall be calculated as the total home price as set forth on
the HUD Closing Statement or the closing disclosure for the sale by Pulte to a third-party
homebuyer of such home, less the first $25,000 of options and commissions. Pursuant to the
Assessment Area 4 Builder Contract, Pulte is responsible for its own home marketing and sales
operation. Pulte has posted a $50,000 letter of credit as a deposit under the Assessment Area 4
Builder Contract, and is expected to post an additional $40,000 deposit in January of 2019. Pulte
is not expected to close on the Assessment Area 4 Contract until the first quarter of 2019.

       The expectations for development discussed herein are based upon estimates and
assumptions made by the Developer that are inherently uncertain, though considered reasonable
by the Developer, and are subject to significant business, economic, and competitive uncertainties
and contingencies, all of which are difficult to predict and many of which are beyond the control
of the Developer and the Builders. As a result, there can be no assurance such absorption rates
will occur or be realized in the time frames anticipated. See " – Residential Product Offerings"
below and "BONDOWNERS' RISKS - Insufficient Resources or Other Factors causing Failure to
Complete the Series 2018 Project or the Development of, and Construction of Homes" herein.

Amenities; Commercial/Retail Development

       The development of Assessment Area 1 in Babcock Ranch Community includes significant
amenity, commercial and retail developments that began in the second quarter of 2016 and is
expected to be substantially complete in early 2020. The total costs of the amenity, commercial
and retail improvements costs approximately $35,000,000, $33,800,000 of which the Developer



                                               70

has funded to date. The residential units in Assessment Area 2B will utilize the amenities in
Assessment Area 1.

         Upon completion, the amenities in Assessment Area 1 will include, without limitation, an
approximately 2,400 square foot lake house with community kitchen, party and game rooms, a
recreational and resort pool, covered outdoor barbeque and kitchen and television area, a lakeside
tot lot, a lakeside lawn, community gardens, multiple walking and biking paths, a dog park, and a
lakeside sunset deck.




       In addition, Assessment Area 1 includes commercial and retail development in the "Town
Square," which provides several additional amenities and is comprised of the following five
buildings: (i) Table & Tap, (ii) Woodlea Hall, (iii) Slaters Goods and Provisions ("Slaters")/Square
Scoops/The Hatchery, (iv) Babcock Neighborhood School, and (v) the Wellness Center, totaling
66,984 square feet. Approximately $26,500,000 has been spent to date on these buildings.




                      [REMAINDER OF PAGE INTENTIONALLY LEFT BLANK]




                                                71

*The picture directly above is a preliminary photo rendering, and is subject to change.




         Table & Tap is a 3,513-square foot lakeside restaurant and full bar serving lunch and dinner
7 days per week. It features a seasonal menu featuring local farm to table ingredients. Woodlea
Hall is located south of Table & Tap and features three tenants. It totals 10,208 square feet. The
first floor of Woodlea Hall houses the Discovery Center, where visitors can come to learn more



                                                 72

about the Babcock Ranch Community, builders, initiatives and register for more information. Also
on the first floor is a retail shopping outlet Curry Creek Outfitters where visitors can purchase
Babcock Ranch and Curry Creek Outfitters branded merchandise geared for the outdoor lifestyle.
Paddle boards, kayaks, and canoes are available for rent, and cruises of Lake Babcock on the
electric boat can be scheduled in front of Table & Tap. The second floor houses the Developer's
offices at Babcock Ranch. Across the street, to the south of Woodlea Hall is Slaters, Square
Scoops, and The Hatchery. The building totals 14,400 square feet. Slaters is a casual market, café,
and general store that serves breakfast, lunch and dinner. Square Scoops is an ice cream and coffee
shop. The second floor houses The Hatchery, a flexible co-working and event space where visitors
can rent a desk office, or meeting room space in hourly, daily or monthly increments.

       To the east of Slaters, is the previous Babcock Neighborhood School, a 12,456-square foot
building, for which the Developer is currently negotiating opportunities to lease a portion of the
building to a voluntary pre-kindergarten ("VPK") provider, or could convert it into office space.
Additionally, the new K-8 permanent Babcock Neighborhood School building located directly
northwest of Assessment Area 2A was constructed and opened in August 2018. The Babcock
Neighborhood School received an "A" rating for the 2017-2018 school year. The current Babcock
Neighborhood School building could also be used to hold additional classrooms for pre-school or
VPK. See " - Education" below for more information.

       To the north of the school building and the east of Table & Tap on Lake Babcock is the
Wellness Center, which is substantially complete, with additional interior improvements for office
space expecting to be completed by the end of December 2018. The Wellness Center is 26,407
square feet and will house a gym, a physician's office, additional office space and a juice bar.

        The larger Babcock Ranch Community will be surrounded by a 73,000-acre permanent
nature preserve and will contain approximately 9,000 acres of natural greenways, parks, lakes and
over 50 miles of trails. The Developer also anticipates that the Lennar development of Assessment
Area 3A will include an 18-hole golf course.

Development Approvals and Permits

        The District, including the Series 2018 Assessment Areas, is located within the Master
DRI, which is governed by the Master DRI Development Order, initially approved by the County
on December 13, 2007 and subsequently amended, most recently by Resolution No. 2017-187,
adopted on July 25, 2017 (the "Master DO"). The Master DO provides for development of the
District in increments. Assessment Area 2B has all development approvals necessary to install its
allocable portion of the Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project.
Platting of Assessment Area 2B is expected to occur in the first quarter of 2019. Lennar Homes
is responsible for constructing the homes in Assessment Area 2B and obtain certificates of
occupancy. Assessment Areas 3A and 4 are not platted.

Environmental

        URS Corporation Southern ("URS") prepared a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment
dated July 26, 2006 and a Limited Phase II Environmental Site Assessment dated July 12, 2006
(collectively, the "ESAs") on all of the lands in the Babcock Ranch Community (which include



                                                73

lands outside of the Development). The ESAs noted that current and prior uses of the lands in
Babcock Ranch included agricultural fields, cattle grazing fields, hunting camps, an asphalt
production facility and a limestone mining facility. The ESAs determined that levels of arsenic,
dieldrin, atrazine, and petroleum products were present in numerous locations throughout the
Babcock Ranch Community, including several locations within Assessment Area 1, at levels that
exceeded State cleanup target levels. The ESAs recommended that additional site assessments be
performed to determine the extent of any soil or groundwater contamination. Since 2006, the
Developer has conducted further assessments and undertaken various site rehabilitation measures.
There is currently only one site within the Babcock Ranch Community that requires remediation
for elevated levels of arsenic and petroleum, and that site is located in the Lee Development area
outside of, and not adjacent to, the Series 2018 Assessment Areas. A portion of the land in
Assessment Area 4 was previously contaminated and remediated in 2006. See "BONDOWNERS'
RISKS - Regulatory and Environmental Risks" herein for more information regarding potential
environmental risks.

Utilities and the Utility System

         FP&L will be the electric utility provider for the Series 2018 Assessment Areas. The
internal roadways in the Series 2018 Assessment Areas are not paved yet. Potable water, sanitary
sewer, and irrigation quality water will be provided by Babcock Ranch Water Utilities, a
department of the District, through an agreement with MSKP Town and Country Utility, LLC, an
affiliate of the Developer.

        Other Assessments. The District anticipates imposing operations and maintenance
assessments of approximately $375 per unit per year, which are subject to change. The land within
the District has been and is expected to continue to be subject to taxes and assessments imposed
by taxing authorities other than the District. These taxes would be payable in addition to the Series
2018 Assessments and any other assessments levied by the District. In addition, exclusive of voter
approved millages levied for general obligation bonds, as to which no limit applies, Charlotte
County and the School District of Charlotte County, Florida, may each levy ad valorem taxes upon
the land in the Series 2018 Assessment Areas up to 10 mills each. The millage rate applicable to
property located within the Charlotte Development in tax year 2017 was approximately 16.03160


                                                                      75

mills. The District has no control over the level of ad valorem taxes and/or special assessments
levied by other taxing authorities. It is possible that in future years taxes levied by these other
entities could be substantially higher than in the current year.

Education

        School age residents of the Development initially attended East Elementary School, Punta
Gorda Middle School and Charlotte High School, which are located approximately 30 miles, 28
miles and 28 miles away from the Development, respectively. The Developer entered into a
School Site Dedication Agreement with The School Board of Charlotte County (the "School
Board") in February 2009 pursuant to which the Developer agreed to dedicate three 20-acre
elementary school sites, one 30-acre middle school site, one 50-acre high school site and one
25-acre educational service center site. The dedication of school sites will allow the children
within the Development to attend schools within the Development. The School Site Dedication
Agreement was amended on January 9, 2018, which amendment, inter alia, provides that the
parties intend to revisit the School Site Dedication Agreement as further development of the
charter schools occur, recognizing that the charter schools within the Development may eliminate
the need for school and support sites to be dedicated to the School Board.

       The new K-8 permanent Babcock Neighborhood School building was constructed and
opened in August 2018. The Babcock Neighborhood School received an "A" rating for the 2017-
2018 school year. The current Babcock Neighborhood School building could also be used to hold
additional classrooms for pre-school or VPK. The previous Babcock Neighborhood School, a
12,456 square foot building, is currently vacant. The Developer is currently negotiating
opportunities to lease a portion of the building to a VPK provider, or could be convert it into office
space.

Competition

        The scale of the Babcock Ranch Community, combined with its access to nature and
sustainable energy initiatives, is expected to offer residents a unique lifestyle experience within
Southwest Florida. Locally, however, the Babcock Ranch Community is expected to compete
with projects in the County market generally and, more particularly, the southern portion of the
County and the northern portion of Lee County. The Developer believes the projects listed below
offer different lifestyles than the Babcock Ranch Community, but are the closest in proximity for
direct competition with the Development.

       Verandah

       Verandah is located approximately seven miles from the Development and was originally
developed by Bonita Bay Properties. Neal Communities is now the current builder in Verandah.
Development started in Verandah in early 2004. At build out, Verandah is expected to contain




                                                 76

1,587 homes with 36 holes of golf. Home prices are believed to range from approximately
$150,000 to over $950,000.

       Pelican Preserve

        Pelican Preserve is located approximately 14 miles from the Development and was
originally developed by the Worthington Group. Lennar Homes is the builder in Pelican Preserve.
Development started in Pelican Preserve in late 2001. At build out, Pelican Preserve is expected
to contain 2,254 homes and 27 holes of golf. Home prices are believed to range from
approximately $160,000 to over $750,000.

       River Hall

       River Hall is located approximately eight miles from the Development and is being
developed by GreenPointe Communities. Lennar Homes, DR Horton, and Pulte are the builders
in River Hall. Development started in River Hall in 2006. At build out, River Hall is expected to
contain 2,267 homes and an 18-hole golf course. Home prices are believed to range from the
$170,000 to over $500,000.

        The information in this sub-section has been obtained from third parties and public sources
believed to be accurate, but cannot be certified as to its accuracy and is subject to change. This
section does not purport to summarize all of the existing or planned communities in the area of the
Development, but rather provide a description of those that the Developer feels pose primary
competition to the Development.


                                      THE DEVELOPER

         The Developer owns the vast majority of land in the District, including all the land
comprising the Series 2018 Assessment Areas, which is subject to the Series 2018 Special
Assessments. The Developer also owns the remainder of the land in the District, except for certain
lands owned by the Utility System Provider, Ecologic Waste Management, LLC (an affiliate of
the Developer), the District, Charlotte County, other home builders and FP&L. The Developer
was organized on July 25, 2006. The Developer is a single purpose investment entity whose sole
asset is the land it owns in the District.

         The sole member of the Developer is KE Babcock Holdings, LLC, a Delaware limited
liability company, which is in turn owned by Kitson & Partners Communities, LLC, a Delaware
limited company, which is in turn owned by Kitson–Evergreen LLC, a Delaware limited liability
company ("Kitson–Evergreen"). Kitson–Evergreen and its affiliates do business under the name
Kitson & Partners. Kitson–Evergreen is owned by Kitson Investment Partners, LLC, a Delaware
limited liability company ("KIP") and Evergreen Florida Development, LLC, a Delaware limited
liability company ("Evergreen Florida"). Evergreen Florida owns a substantial majority interest
in Kitson & Partners. Kitson & Partners is managed by KIP as the operating member subject to
the oversight of an Executive Committee established to make all required "major decisions." The
Executive Committee is composed of two KIP members and three Evergreen Florida members.




                                                77

        Evergreen Florida is wholly owned by Evergreen Real Estate Partners, LLC ("Evergreen").
Evergreen is the primary real estate principal investment arm of M3 Capital Partners ("M3"),
through which it manages a $5.2 billion real estate private equity investment vehicle for one of the
largest U.S. state pension funds. M3 initiated its principal investment program in 2005 with the
creation of Evergreen, a wholly-owned subsidiary of the firm. Its investment in Kitson–Evergreen
dates back to 2006. Since its inception, Evergreen has invested in 14 specialized real estate
companies in the U.S., Europe, Asia, Brazil and Australia. Evergreen is capitalized by its state
pension fund investor, with a minority co-investment from M3.

        Evergreen has committed an allocation of $430 million to Kitson–Evergreen, subject to
specific approval of proposed investments. It approves specific capital investments for projects
recommended by the Kitson–Evergreen Investment Committee and approved by the
Kitson-Evergreen Executive Committee.              Contractual obligations undertaken by
Kitson-Evergreen, such as the transactions contemplated by and required to close the Series 2018
Bonds, are each approved as a "major decision" and authorized by the Kitson–Evergreen Executive
Committee before the funds required to perform such contractual obligations are specifically
allocated and committed.

        Kitson & Partners was founded in 2000, although its management team's experience dates
back to 1986 and is set forth in more detail below. Kitson & Partners' company headquarters are
located in Palm Beach Gardens, Florida. In 2010, Kitson & Partners completed the development
of the Ibis Golf & Country Club, a 1,871 single family home residential development that included
three 18-hole golf courses and a 50,000 square foot clubhouse in West Palm Beach, Florida.
Kitson & Partners also co-developed the Jack Nicklaus Golf Club of Korea, an 18-hole Nicklaus
Signature golf course and 80,000 square foot clubhouse in a development known as the New
Songdo International City in Incheon Metropolitan City, Korea. In 2016, Kitson & Partners
completed the development of Bay Pines, a mixed-use project near St. Petersburg, Florida
consisting of 56 single-family homes, 330 apartments, 218,000 square feet of commercial space
and a 125-room hotel. Kitson & Partners is also developing Talis Park, an approximately 461-acre
master-planned residential community primarily located in the northwestern portion of
unincorporated Collier County, Florida that is planned for approximately 590 homes and an 18-
hole golf course and related amenities.

        The biographies of the key principals and management team of the Developer, which are
also the key principals and management team of KIP, are below.

       Sydney W. "Syd" Kitson, Chief Executive Officer. Syd Kitson is a graduate of Wake
Forest University with a Bachelor of Arts degree in Economics. Kitson had a notable career in the
National Football League, playing offensive guard for both the Green Bay Packers and the Dallas
Cowboys. Upon his retirement from professional football, he began his career in real estate by
founding a company that, from 1986 to 1992, was responsible for the development and sale of
residential communities, commercial properties, retail stores, medical offices and senior housing
units.

       In 1992, he helped create the residential division of Gale & Wentworth, and later, in 1996,
became its President and Chief Operating Officer. From 1996 through 1999, he was responsible
for overseeing the development of numerous successful master-planned communities, including


                                                78

Cherry Valley (Montgomery Township, New Jersey) and Ibis Golf & Country Club (West Palm
Beach, Florida). He founded Kitson & Partners in 2000.

        Mr. Kitson lectures at Princeton University, and has served on multiple civic, education
and non-profit boards. In addition to his appointment as a member of the Board of Governors for
the State University System of Florida, he is currently on the Executive Committee and Board of
Directors of the Florida Council of 100, as well as the immediate past Chairman for the Florida
Chamber of Commerce. He is a member of the NFL Alumni Association and the National Football
League Players' Association. In 2000, he received the Rainbow Award from the Adam Walsh
Children's Foundation. Mr. Kitson also serves as the Chairman and CEO of KIP and is a founding
partner of KIP.

        Rick Severance, President. In his role as President of the Developer, Rick Severance brings
over twenty years of experience in community development, organizational utilization and
financial performance to the Kitson & Partners team.

        Prior to joining Kitson & Partners, Mr. Severance was the SVP of Planning, Development
and Operations for the St. Joe Company and previously the CEO of New Vector Limited, a highly
successful consulting firm for numerous new urban and master planned resort communities and
commercial town centers. In prior years, Mr. Severance has worked with national lodging brands,
real estate development companies, and large REITs as Consulting Senior Manager in the
hospitality and real estate practice of Ernst & Young, LLC. His consulting career was preceded
by his position as CEO of the Seaside Community Development Company, responsible for the
direction and development of Seaside, Florida, the award-winning town named TIME Magazine's
"Development of the Decade."

         Mr. Severance has addressed the Urban Land Institute ("ULI"), the American Society for
Training and Development, the Hospitality Asset Managers Association and the Florida State
University's MBA program. He is a passionate advocate for "charity water," a non-profit
organization dedicated to supplying fresh water to those in need in Africa. Mr. Severance is active
in raising awareness of the critical need for safe, clean drinking water in third world countries. He
completed a 70.3 Ironman Triathlon to support the group's efforts, raising funds to build two new
fresh water wells in Ethiopia.

        Thomas Hoban, Vice President. Thomas Hoban is President, Chief Investment Officer and
Partner of Kitson & Partners, and Vice President of KE Babcock Holdings, LLC. Mr. Hoban has
20 years of experience in the real estate industry, which includes a broad understanding of the
capital markets. He helps set the strategic direction for K&P and is responsible for the financial
division of the company. Mr. Hoban spends the majority of his time focusing on growth initiatives
for K&P, both through investment and capital sourcing. In addition, he is responsible for K&P's
1.5M square foot retail shopping center portfolio and its development pipeline of approximately
800,000 square feet of retail. He played a key role in procuring and structuring K&P's entity-level
investment with Evergreen and was also instrumental in structuring and executing the purchase of
the Babcock Florida Company.

       Prior to joining the K&P team in 2003, Mr. Hoban spent eight years in New York City as
an investment banker at Morgan Stanley, where he served as Vice President of U.S. Acquisitions


                                                 79

for the Morgan Stanley Real Estate Fund ("MSREF"). During his career on Wall Street, Mr.
Hoban worked on both the agency and principal side of the real estate business. While working
for MSREF, he spearheaded the formation of Morgan Stanley's joint venture with Kitson &
Partners.

       Mr. Hoban graduated cum laude from University of Notre Dame with a Bachelor of
Business Administration in Finance. He is an active member in the ULI and the International
Council of Shopping Centers.

        Kitson–Evergreen Advisory Board. Kitson–Evergreen also has an Advisory Board, which
meets three times a year to review performance and strategic initiatives. The Advisory Board
currently consists of five members: Lawrence Burns, Jeb Bush, Peter Rummell, William Smith,
