                                                      D-2

1.0   Introduction


      1.1   Purpose

            This Master Special Assessment Methodology Report for Phase
            2B, 3A, and 4 (the “Report”) was developed to provide a master
            financing plan for the Babcock Ranch Community Independent
            Special District’s (the “District”) Phases 2B, 3A, and 4 (hereinafter
            collectively referred to as “Phase III”) related to funding the costs of
            the acquisition and construction of public infrastructure
            improvements contemplated to be provided by the District in Phase
            III. The District is located in Charlotte and Lee Counties in Florida,
            and was established by House Bill 1515 (codified in Chapter 2007-
            306, Laws of Florida) passed by the Florida Legislature and
            approved by the Governor of the State of Florida on June 27, 2007.
            House Bill 1039 (codified as amended by in Chapter 2016-257,
            Laws of Florida), added additional acreage (Chapter 2007-306 and
            2016-257 together the “Act”). The District has a total of 17,787
            acres of land, with Phase III comprising 560 +/- acres located in
            Charlotte County.

      1.2   Scope of the Report

            This Report presents the projections for financing the District’s
            public infrastructure improvements (the “Capital Improvement
            Program”) for Phase III as described in the Engineer’s Report of
            Kimley-Horn & Associates, Inc. dated December 5, 2018 (the
            “Engineer's Report”), and describes the method for the allocation of
            special benefits and the apportionment of special assessment debt
            resulting from the provision and funding of the Capital Improvement
            Program. The Capital Improvement Program is comprised of the
            Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
            Project, as described in the Engineer’s Report.

      1.3   Special Benefits and General Benefits

            Improvements undertaken and funded by the District as part of the
            Capital Improvement Program create special and peculiar benefits,
            different in kind and degree than general benefits, for properties
            within Phase III, as well as general benefits for properties within the
            District’s borders but outside Phase III, and general benefits to the
            public at large. However, as discussed within this Report, these
            general benefits are incidental in nature and are readily
            distinguishable from the special and peculiar benefits which accrue


                                             1
                                       D-3

      to property within Phase III. Phase III’s Capital Improvement
      Program enables properties within its boundaries to be developed.

      There is no doubt that the general public and owners of property
      within the District but outside of Phase III, and outside the District
      will benefit from the provision of the Capital Improvement Program.
      However, these benefits are only incidental since the Capital
      Improvement Program is designed solely to provide special benefits
      peculiar to property within Phase III. Properties outside of Phase III
      are not directly served by the Capital Improvement Program and do
      not depend upon the Capital Improvement Program to obtain or to
      maintain their development entitlements. This fact alone clearly
      distinguishes the special benefits which Phase III properties receive
      compared to those lying outside of the Phase III’s boundaries.

      The Capital Improvement Program will provide the public
      infrastructure improvements necessary to make the Phase III lands
      within the District developable and saleable. The installation of
      such improvements will cause the value of the developable and
      saleable lands within Phase III to increase by more than the sum of
      the financed cost of the individual components of the Capital
      Improvement Program. Even though the exact value of the benefits
      provided by the Phase III Capital Improvement Program is hard to
      estimate at this point, it is nevertheless greater than the costs
      associated with providing the same.

1.4   Organization of the Report

      Section Two describes the development program as proposed by
      the Developer, as defined below.

      Section Three provides a summary of the Capital Improvement
      Program as determined by the District Engineer.

      Section Four discusses the financing program for the District.

      Section Five introduces the special assessment methodology for
      Phase III of the District.




                                      2
                                D-4

2.0   Development Program


      2.1   Overview

            Phase III will serve a portion of the Babcock Ranch development
            which is a residential, master planned development located in
            Charlotte and Lee Counties, Florida. According to the District
            Engineer, Phase 2B has an area of approximately 85.8 +/- acres,
            Phase 3A has an area of approximately 400.1 +/- acres, and Phase
            4 has an area of approximately 74.1 +/- acres for a total of 560 +/-
            acres and is generally located in Charlotte County north of the
            Charlotte/Lee County line and east of State Road 31. The District
            currently consists of approximately 17,787 +/- acres and is
            generally located to the east of Florida State Road 31 and in both
            Charlotte and Lee Counties.

      2.2   The Development Program

            The development of Phase III is anticipated to be conducted by
            Babcock Property Holdings, LLC or its associates (the
            “Developer”). Three separate assessment areas: Trails Edge 2 or
            Phase 2B (“Phase 2B” or “Assessment Area 2B”) with a total area
            of approximately 85.8 +/- acres, Babcock National or Phase 3A
            (“Phase 3A” or “Assessment Area 3A”) with a total area of
            approximately 400.1 +/- acres, and Earthsource or Phase 4 (“Phase
            4” or “Assessment Area 4”) with a total area of approximately 74.1
            +/- acres, have been created to facilitate the financing and the
            Developer’s development plan. Phase 2B envisions a total of 243
            residential units, Phase 3A envisions a total of 1,053 residential
            units, and Phase 4 envisions a total of 166 residential units
            although land use types and unit numbers may change throughout
            the development period. Table 1 in the Appendix illustrates the
            development plan for Phase III.


3.0   The Capital Improvement Program


      3.1   Overview

            The public infrastructure costs to be funded by the District are
            described by the District Engineer in the Engineer's Report. Only
            public infrastructure that may qualify for bond financing by the
            District under the Act and under the Internal Revenue Code of
            1986, as amended, was included in these estimates.

                                           3
                                     D-5

3.2   Capital Improvement Program

      The Capital Improvement Program needed to serve the
      Development is projected to consist of roadways, clearing and
      grading, mitigation, offsite improvements, utilities, storm water
      management, landscaping, and entrance features. As described in
      the Engineer’s Report, the Capital Improvement Program is
      comprised of the Series 2018 Master Project, which provides the
      master infrastructure for all three phases within the Phase III, and
      the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project, which provides the
      neighborhood infrastructure for Phase 2B only. The costs of the
      Series 2018 Master Project have been allocated by the District
      Engineer in accordance with each phase’s respective projected use
      and proportionate share of the Series 2018 Master Project
      improvements. As the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project
      includes internal improvements specific to Phase 2B only, the costs
      of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project have been allocated
      by the District Engineer to the Phase 2B only.

      In Phase 2B, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 2B
      and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
      one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
      Improvement Program for Phase 2B are estimated at
      $3,586,869.84 for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project and
      an additional $1,520,000 for the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
      Project, the Phase 2B parcel specific costs, for a total of
      $5,106,869.84. Table 2 in the Appendix illustrates the specific
      components of the Capital Improvement Program and their costs.
      The District intends to finance a portion of such costs with bonds.

      In Phase 3A, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 3A
      and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
      one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
      Improvement Program for Phase 3A are estimated at
      $14,544,691.44 for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project.
      Table 2 in the Appendix illustrates the specific components of the
      Capital Improvement Program and their costs. The District intends
      to finance all of such costs with bonds.

      In Phase 4, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 4

                                       4
                                 D-6

            and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
            one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
            Improvement Program for Phase 4 are estimated at $3,140,438.72
            for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project. Table 2 in the
            Appendix illustrates the specific components of the Capital
            Improvement Program and their costs. The District intends to
            finance a portion of such costs with bonds.


4.0   Financing Program


      4.1   Overview

            As noted above, Phase III is embarking on a program of capital
            improvements which will facilitate the development of lands within
            Phase III. Generally, construction of public improvements is either
            funded by the Developer and then acquired by the District or
            funded directly by the District. The choice of the exact mechanism
            for providing public infrastructure has not yet been made at the time
            of this writing, and the District may either acquire the public
            infrastructure from the Developer or construct it, or even partly
            acquire it and partly construct it.

            Even though the actual financing plan may change, this Report
            provides a master financing plan under which the District would
            issue approximately $5,585,000 in par amount of special
            assessment bonds for Phase 2B (the "Assessment Area 2B
            Bonds") to fund approximately $4,786,828.45 in the Phase 2B
            portion of the Series 2018 Master Project and Series 2018
            Neighborhood 2B Project costs, approximately $19,450,000 in par
            amount of special assessment bonds for Phase 3A (the
            "Assessment Area 3A Bonds") to fund approximately
            $14,544,691.44 in the Phase 3A portion of the Series 2018 Master
            Project, and approximately $3,385,000 in par amount of special
            assessment bonds for Phase 4 (the "Assessment Area 4 Bonds",
            and cumulatively with the Assessment Area 2B Bonds and
            Assessment Area 3A Bonds, the “Bonds”) to fund approximately
            $2,901,238.02 in the Phase 4 portion of the Series 2018 Master
            Project, and would also include funding for capitalized interest, a
            debt service reserve account, and costs of issuance.

            Please note that the purpose of this Report is to allocate the
            benefit of the Capital Improvement Program to the various
            land uses within the Phase 2B, 3A, and 4 Assessment Areas
            based on such benefit allocation to apportion the maximum

                                             5
                                       D-7

      amount of debt necessary to fund the Capital Improvement
      Program. The discussion of the structure and size of the
      indebtedness is based on various estimates and is subject to
      change.

4.2   Types of Bonds Proposed

      The proposed master financing plan for Phase 2B within the District
      provides for the issuance of the Assessment Area 2B Bonds in the
      approximate principal amount of $5,585,000 to finance costs
      estimated at $4,786,828.45. The Assessment Area 2B Bonds as
      projected under this master financing plan would be structured to
      be amortized in 30 annual installments following an 11-month
      capitalized interest period. Interest payments on the Assessment
      Area 2B Bonds would be made every May 1 and November 1, and
      principal payments on the Assessment Area 2B Bonds would be
      made every November 1.

      In order to finance the improvement costs for Phase 2B, the District
      would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the
      total amount of $5,585,000. The difference is comprised of debt
      service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
      including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
      of funding for the Assessment Area 2B Bonds are presented in
      Table 3 in the Appendix.

      The proposed master financing plan for Phase 3A within the District
      provides for the issuance of the Assessment Area 3A Bonds in the
      approximate principal amount of $19,450,000 to finance costs
      estimated at $14,544,691.44. The Assessment Area 3A Bonds as
      projected under this master financing plan would be structured to
      be amortized in 27 annual installments following a 35-month
      capitalized interest period. Interest payments on the Assessment
      Area 3A Bonds would be made every May 1 and November 1, and
      principal payments on the Assessment Area 3A Bonds would be
      made every November 1.

      In order to finance the improvement costs Phase 3A, the District
      would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the
      total amount of $19,450,000. The difference is comprised of debt
      service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
      including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
      of funding for the Assessment Area 3A Bonds are presented in
      Table 3 in the Appendix.



                                     6
                               D-8

            The proposed master financing plan for Phase 4 within the District
            provides for the issuance of the Assessment Area 4 Bonds in the
            approximate principal amount of $3,385,000 to finance costs
            estimated at $2,901,238.02. The Assessment Area 4 Bonds as
            projected under this master financing plan would be structured to
            be amortized in 30 annual installments following an 11-month
            capitalized interest period. Interest payments on the Assessment
            Area 4 Bonds would be made every May 1 and November 1, and
            principal payments on the Assessment Area 4 Bonds would be
            made every November 1.

            In order to finance the improvement costs Phase 4, the District
            would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the
            total amount of $3,385,000. The difference is comprised of debt
            service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
            including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
            of funding for the Assessment Area 4 Bonds are presented in Table
            3 in the Appendix.

            Please note that the structure of the Bonds as presented in
            this Report is preliminary and may change due to changes in
            the development program, market conditions, timing of
            infrastructure installation as well as for other reasons. The
            District maintains complete flexibility as to the structure of the
            Bonds and reserves the right to modify it as necessary.


5.0   Assessment Methodology


      5.1   Overview

            The issuance of the Bonds provides the District with funds
            necessary to construct/acquire the Capital Improvement Program
            outlined in Section 3.2 and described in more detail by the District
            Engineer in the Engineer's Report. These improvements yield
            special and general benefits, with special benefits accruing to
            properties within the boundaries of Phase III and general benefits
            accruing to areas outside of Phase III and outside the District and
            being only incidental in nature. The debt incurred in financing the
            public infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement Program
            will be paid off by assessing properties that derive special and
            peculiar benefits from the Capital Improvement Program. All
            properties that receive special benefits from the Capital
            Improvement Program will be assessed for their fair share of the
            debt issued in order to finance the Capital Improvement Program.

                                           7
                                     D-9

5.2   Benefit Allocation

      The current development plan envisions the development of a total
      of 1,462 residential units, with 243 residential units for Phase 2B,
      1,053 residential units for Phase 3A, and 166 residential units for
      Phase 4, although unit numbers and land use types may change
      throughout the development period.

      As described in the Engineer’s Report, the Capital Improvement
      Program is comprised of the Series 2018 Master Project, which
      provides the master infrastructure for all three phases within the
      Phase III, and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project, which
      provides the neighborhood infrastructure for Phase 2B only. The
      costs of the Series 2018 Master Project have been allocated by the
      District Engineer in accordance with each phase’s respective
      projected use and proportionate share of the Series 2018 Master
      Project improvements. As the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
      Project includes internal improvements specific to Phase 2B only,
      the costs of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project have been
      allocated by the District Engineer to the Phase 2B only.

      The public infrastructure included in the Series 2018 Master Project
      portion of the Capital Improvement Program will comprise an
      interrelated system of improvements within respectively
      Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
      4, which means that all of the improvements will serve respectively
      Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
      4, and such public improvements will be interrelated such that they
      will reinforce each other and their combined benefits will be greater
      than the sum of their individual benefits. All of the land uses within
      Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
      4 will benefit from each infrastructure improvement category, as
      respectively the Series 2018 Master Project portion of the Capital
      Improvement Program provides basic infrastructure to all lands
      within Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and
      Assessment Area 4 and benefits all lands within respectively
      Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
      4 as an integrated system of improvements.

      Similarly, the public infrastructure included in the Series 2018
      Neighborhood 2B Project will comprise an interrelated system of
      improvements within Assessment Area 2B, which means that all of
      the improvements will serve Assessment Area 2B and such public
      improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce each
      other and their combined benefits will be greater than the sum of
      their individual benefits. All of the land uses within Assessment

                                      8
                               D-10

Area 2B will benefit from each infrastructure improvement category,
as respectively the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project portion of
the Capital Improvement Program provides basic infrastructure to
all lands within Assessment Area 2B and benefits all lands within
Assessment Area 2B as an integrated system of improvements.

As stated previously, the Capital Improvement Program has a
logical connection to the special and peculiar benefits received by
the land within Phase 2B, Phase 3A, and Phase 4, as without such
improvements, the development of the properties within Phase 2B,
Phase 3A, and Phase 4 would not be possible. Based upon the
connection between the improvements and the special and peculiar
benefits to the land within Phase 2B, Phase 3A, and Phase 4, the
District can assign or allocate a portion of the District's debt through
the imposition of non-ad valorem assessments, to the land
receiving such special and peculiar benefits. Even though these
special and peculiar benefits are real and ascertainable, the precise
amount of the benefit cannot yet be calculated with mathematical
certainty. However, such benefit is more valuable than the cost of,
or the actual non-ad valorem assessment amount levied on that
parcel.

Phase 2B is projected to be developed with a total of 243
residential units and the benefit associated with the Phase 2B
portion of the Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital
Improvement Program as well as the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
Project component of the Capital Improvement Program is
proposed to be allocated to units within Phase 2B in proportion to
the density of development and intensity of use of infrastructure as
measured by a standard unit called an Equivalent Residential Unit
("ERU"). Table 4 in the Appendix illustrates the ERU weights that
are proposed to be assigned to the land uses contemplated to be
developed within Phase 2B based on the densities of development
and the intensities of use of infrastructure, the total ERU counts for
each land use category, for each phase and the share of the benefit
received by each land use within each phase.

Phase 3A is projected to be developed with a total of 1,053
residential units and the benefit associated with the Phase 3A
portion of the Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital
Improvement Program is proposed to be allocated to units within
Phase 3A in proportion to the density of development and intensity
of use of infrastructure as measured by the ERU. Table 4 in the
Appendix illustrates the ERU weights that are proposed to be
assigned to the land uses contemplated to be developed within
Phase 3A based on the densities of development and the

                                 9
                          D-11

intensities of use of infrastructure, the total ERU counts for each
land use category, for each phase and the share of the benefit
received by each land use within each phase.

Phase 4 is projected to be developed with a total of 134 residential
units and the benefit associated with the Phase 4 portion of the
Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital Improvement
Program is proposed to be allocated to units within Phase 4 in
proportion to the density of development and intensity of use of
infrastructure as measured by the ERU. Table 4 in the Appendix
illustrates the ERU weights that are proposed to be assigned to the
land uses contemplated to be developed within Phase 4 based on
the densities of development and the intensities of use of
infrastructure, the total ERU counts for each land use category, for
each phase and the share of the benefit received by each land use
within each phase.

The rationale behind different ERU weights is supported by the fact
that generally and on average smaller units will use and benefit
from the District's infrastructure improvements less than larger
units, as for instance, generally and on average smaller units
produce less storm water runoff, may produce fewer vehicular trips,
and may need less water/sewer capacity than larger units.
Additionally, the value of the larger units is likely to appreciate by
more in terms of dollars than that of the smaller units as a result of
the implementation of the infrastructure improvements. As the
exact amount of the benefit and appreciation is not possible to be
calculated at this time, the use of ERU measures serves as a
reasonable approximation of the relative amount of benefit received
from the District's Capital Improvement Program.

In order to facilitate the marketing of the residential units within
Phase III, the Developer requested that the District limit the amount
of annual assessments for debt service on the Bonds to certain
predetermined levels. Table 5 presents the allocation of the Capital
Improvement Program costs in each assessment area based on
the ERU benefit allocation factors present in Table 4 in the
Appendix. Further, Table 5 illustrates the approximate costs of the
Capital Improvement Program for each assessment area that are
projected to be financed with the Bonds, and the approximate costs
of the Capital Improvement Program to be contributed by the
Developer. In order to accomplish that goal, the Developer will
contribute infrastructure improvements valued at $320,041.39 for
Phase 2B and $239,200.70 for Phase 4 as indicated in Table 5.
Table 6 in the Appendix presents the apportionment of the
assessment associated with the Bonds (the “Bond Assessment”) in

                                10
                         D-12

      accordance with the ERU benefit allocation method presented in
      Table 4 in the Appendix as modified by the effects of Developer’s
      contributions of infrastructure improvements. Table 6 in the
      Appendix also presents the annual levels of the projected annual
      debt service assessments per unit.


5.3   Assigning Bond Assessment

      In Phase 2B, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 2B Bonds (the “Assessment Area 2B Bond
      Assessment”) will be levied initially on approximately 85.8 +/- gross
      acres within the Assessment Area 2B on an equal pro-rata gross
      acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $5,585,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 85.8 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $65,093.24 per acre.

      In Phase 3A, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 3A Bonds (the “Assessment Area 3A Bond
      Assessment”) and will be levied initially on approximately 400.1 +/-
      gross acres within the Assessment Area 3A on an equal pro-rata
      gross acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $19,450,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 400.1 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $48,612.85 per acre.

      In Phase 4, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 4 Bonds (the “Assessment Area 4 Bond
      Assessment”) and will be levied initially on approximately 74.1 +/-
      gross acres within the Assessment Area 4 on an equal pro-rata
      gross acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $3,385,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 74.1 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $45,681.51 per acre.

      As parcels are platted, the Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment,
      Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment, and Assessment Area 4
      Bond Assessment (cumulatively, the “Bond Assessment”) will be
      allocated to such parcels that are platted based on the planned use
      for that parcel as reflected in Table 6 in the Appendix.

      Further, to the extent that any parcel that has not been platted, is
      sold to another developer or builder, the Bond Assessment will be
      assigned to such parcel at the time of the sale based upon the
      development rights associated with such parcel that are transferred
      from seller to buyer. The District shall provide an estoppel or
      similar document to the buyer evidencing the amount of Bond
      Assessment transferred at sale.

                                      11
                               D-13

5.4   Lienability Test: Special and Peculiar Benefit to the Property

      As first discussed in Section 1.3, Special Benefits and General
      Benefits, the Capital Improvement Program creates special and
      peculiar benefits to properties within Phase III. The Capital
      Improvement Program benefits assessable properties within Phase
      III and accrues to all such assessable properties on an ERU basis
      as illustrated in Table 4 in the Appendix.

      The Capital Improvement Program undertaken by the District for
      properties within Phase III can be shown to be creating special and
      peculiar benefits to the property within Phase III. The special and
      peculiar benefits resulting from each improvement are:
             a.     added use of the property;
             b.     added enjoyment of the property;
             c.     decreased insurance premiums; and
             d.     increased marketability and value of the property.

      The Capital Improvement Program makes the land in Phase 2B,
      3A, and 4 developable and saleable and provides special and
      peculiar benefits which are greater than the benefits of any single
      category of improvements. These special and peculiar benefits are
      real and ascertainable, but not yet capable of being calculated and
      assessed in terms of numerical value; however, such benefits are
      more valuable than either the cost of, or the actual assessment
      levied for, the improvement or debt allocated to the parcel of land.

5.5   Lienability Test: Reasonable and Fair Apportionment of the
      Duty to Pay

      A reasonable estimate of the proportion of special and peculiar
      benefits received from the Capital Improvement Program is
      delineated in Table 4 (expressed as ERU factors) in the Appendix.

      The apportionment of the assessments is fair and reasonable
      because it was conducted on the basis of consistent application of
      the methodology described in Section 5.2 across all assessable
      property within the District according to reasonable estimates of the
      special and peculiar benefits derived from the Capital Improvement
      Program by different land uses.

      Accordingly, no acre or parcel of property within the District will be
      liened for the payment of any non-ad valorem special assessment
      more than the determined special benefit peculiar to that property.


                                      12
                               D-14

      5.6   True-Up Mechanism

            The Assessment Methodology described herein is based on
            conceptual information obtained from the Developer prior to
            construction. As development occurs it is possible that the number
            of ERUs may change. The mechanism for maintaining the
            methodology over the changes is referred to as true-up.

            This mechanism is to be utilized to ensure that the Bond
            Assessment on a per ERU basis never exceeds the initially
            allocated assessment as contemplated in the adopted assessment
            methodology. Please note that the True-Up Mechanism will be
            described in detail in the supplemental methodology.

      5.7   Preliminary Assessment Roll

            The preliminary assessment roll is included in Exhibit A.


6.0   Additional Stipulations


      6.1   Overview

            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC was retained by the District to
            prepare a methodology to fairly allocate the special assessments
            related to the District’s Capital Improvement Program. Certain
            financing, development and engineering data was provided by
            members of District Staff and/or the Developer. The allocation
            Methodology described herein was based on information provided
            by those professionals. Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC makes
            no representations regarding said information transactions beyond
            restatement of the factual information necessary for compilation of
            this report. For additional information on the Bond structure and
            related items, please refer to the Offering Statement associated
            with this transaction.

            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC does not represent the
            District as a Municipal Advisor or Securities Broker nor is
            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC registered to provide such
            services as described in Section 15B of the Securities and
            Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. Similarly, Wrathell, Hunt
            and Associates, LLC does not provide the District with
            financial advisory services or offer investment advice in any
            form.

                                                     D-30

1.0   Introduction


      1.1   Purpose

            This Supplemental Special Assessment Methodology Report for
            Phase 2B, 3A, and 4 (the “Report”) was developed to supplement
            the Master Special Assessment Methodology Report for Phase 2B,
            3A, and 4 (the “Original Report”) dated October 10, 2018 and to
            provide a supplemental financing plan for the Babcock Ranch
            Community Independent Special District’s (the “District”) Phases
            2B, 3A, and 4 (hereinafter collectively referred to as “Phase III”)
            related to funding a portion of the costs of the acquisition and
            construction of public infrastructure improvements contemplated to
            be provided by the District in Phase III. The District is located in
            Charlotte and Lee Counties in Florida, and was established by
            House Bill 1515 (codified in Chapter 2007-306, Laws of Florida)
            passed by the Florida Legislature and approved by the Governor of
            the State of Florida on June 27, 2007. House Bill 1039 (codified as
            amended by in Chapter 2016-257, Laws of Florida), added
            additional acreage (Chapter 2007-306 and 2016-257 together the
            “Act”). The District has a total of 17,787 acres of land, with Phase
            III comprising 560 +/- acres located in Charlotte County.

      1.2   Scope of the Report

            This Report presents the projections for financing a portion of the
            District’s public infrastructure improvements (the “Capital
            Improvement Program”) for Phase III as described in the Engineer’s
            Report of Kimley-Horn & Associates, Inc. dated December 5, 2018
            (the “Engineer's Report”), and describes the method for the
            allocation of special benefits and the apportionment of special
            assessment debt resulting from the provision and funding of the
            Capital Improvement Program. The Capital Improvement Program
            is comprised of the Series 2018 Master Project and the Series 2018
            Neighborhood 2B Project, as described in the Engineer’s Report.

      1.3   Special Benefits and General Benefits

            Improvements undertaken and funded in part by the District as part
            of the Capital Improvement Program create special and peculiar
            benefits, different in kind and degree than general benefits, for
            properties within Phase III, as well as general benefits for
            properties within the District’s borders but outside Phase III, and
            general benefits to the public at large. However, as discussed
            within this Report, these general benefits are incidental in nature

                                            1
                                     D-31

      and are readily distinguishable from the special and peculiar
      benefits which accrue to property within Phase III. Phase III’s
      Capital Improvement Program enables properties within its
      boundaries to be developed.

      There is no doubt that the general public and owners of property
      within the District but outside of Phase III, and outside the District
      will benefit from the provision of the Capital Improvement Program.
      However, these benefits are only incidental since the Capital
      Improvement Program is designed solely to provide special benefits
      peculiar to property within Phase III. Properties outside of Phase III
      are not directly served by the Capital Improvement Program and do
      not depend upon the Capital Improvement Program to obtain or to
      maintain their development entitlements. This fact alone clearly
      distinguishes the special benefits which Phase III properties receive
      compared to those lying outside of the Phase III’s boundaries.

      The Capital Improvement Program will provide the public
      infrastructure improvements necessary to make the Phase III lands
      within the District developable and saleable. The installation of
      such improvements will cause the value of the developable and
      saleable lands within Phase III to increase by more than the sum of
      the financed cost of the individual components of the Capital
      Improvement Program. Even though the exact value of the benefits
      provided by the Phase III Capital Improvement Program is hard to
      estimate at this point, it is nevertheless greater than the costs
      associated with providing the same.

1.4   Organization of the Report

      Section Two describes the development program as proposed by
      the Developer, as defined below.

      Section Three provides a summary of the Capital Improvement
      Program as determined by the District Engineer.

      Section Four discusses the financing program for the District.

      Section Five introduces the special assessment methodology for
      Phase III of the District.




                                      2
                               D-32

2.0   Development Program


      2.1   Overview

            Phase III will serve a portion of the Babcock Ranch development
            which is a residential, master planned development located in
            Charlotte and Lee Counties, Florida. According to the District
            Engineer, Phase 2B has an area of approximately 85.8 +/- acres,
            Phase 3A has an area of approximately 400.1 +/- acres, and Phase
            4 has an area of approximately 74.1 +/- acres for a total of 560 +/-
            acres and is generally located in Charlotte County north of the
            Charlotte/Lee County line and east of State Road 31. The District
            currently consists of approximately 17,787 +/- acres and is
            generally located to the east of Florida State Road 31 and in both
            Charlotte and Lee Counties.

      2.2   The Development Program

            The development of Phase III is anticipated to be conducted by
            Babcock Property Holdings, LLC or its associates (the
            “Developer”). Three separate assessment areas: Trails Edge 2 or
            Phase 2B (“Phase 2B” or “Assessment Area 2B”) with a total area
            of approximately 85.8 +/- acres, Babcock National or Phase 3A
            (“Phase 3A” or “Assessment Area 3A”) with a total area of
            approximately 400.1 +/- acres, and Earthsource or Phase 4 (“Phase
            4” or “Assessment Area 4”) with a total area of approximately 74.1
            +/- acres, have been created to facilitate the financing and the
            Developer’s development plan. Phase 2B envisions a total of 243
            residential units, Phase 3A envisions a total of 1,053 residential
            units, and Phase 4 envisions a total of 166 residential units
            although land use types and unit numbers may change throughout
            the development period. Table 1 in the Appendix illustrates the
            development plan for Phase III.


3.0   The Capital Improvement Program


      3.1   Overview

            The public infrastructure costs to be funded by the District are
            described by the District Engineer in the Engineer's Report. Only
            public infrastructure that may qualify for bond financing by the
            District under the Act and under the Internal Revenue Code of
            1986, as amended, was included in these estimates.

                                            3
                                     D-33

3.2   Capital Improvement Program

      The Capital Improvement Program needed to serve the
      Development is projected to consist of roadways, clearing and
      grading, mitigation, offsite improvements, utilities, storm water
      management, landscaping, and entrance features. As described in
      the Engineer’s Report, the Capital Improvement Program is
      comprised of the Series 2018 Master Project, which provides the
      master infrastructure for all three phases within the Phase III, and
      the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project, which provides the
      neighborhood infrastructure for Phase 2B only. The costs of the
      Series 2018 Master Project have been allocated by the District
      Engineer in accordance with each phase’s respective projected use
      and proportionate share of the Series 2018 Master Project
      improvements. As the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project
      includes internal improvements specific to Phase 2B only, the costs
      of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project have been allocated
      by the District Engineer to the Phase 2B only.

      In Phase 2B, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 2B
      and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
      one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
      Improvement Program for Phase 2B are estimated at
      $3,586,869.84 for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project and
      an additional $1,520,000 for the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
      Project, the Phase 2B parcel specific costs, for a total of
      $5,106,869.84. Table 2 in the Appendix illustrates the specific
      components of the Capital Improvement Program and their costs.
      The District intends to finance a portion of such costs with bonds.

      In Phase 3A, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 3A
      and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
      one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
      Improvement Program for Phase 3A are estimated at
      $14,544,691.44 for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project.
      Table 2 in the Appendix illustrates the specific components of the
      Capital Improvement Program and their costs. The District intends
      to finance a portion of such costs with bonds.

      In Phase 4, the infrastructure included in the Capital Improvement
      Program will comprise an interrelated system of improvements,
      which means all of the improvements will serve the entire Phase 4

                                       4
                                D-34

            and improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce
            one another. At the time of this writing, the total costs of the Capital
            Improvement Program for Phase 4 are estimated at $3,140,438.72
            for its portion of the Series 2018 Master Project. Table 2 in the
            Appendix illustrates the specific components of the Capital
            Improvement Program and their costs. The District intends to
            finance a portion of such costs with bonds.


4.0   Financing Program


      4.1   Overview

            As noted above, Phase III is embarking on a program of capital
            improvements which will facilitate the development of lands within
            Phase III. Generally, construction of public improvements is either
            funded by the Developer and then acquired by the District or
            funded directly by the District. The choice of the exact mechanism
            for providing public infrastructure has not yet been made at the time
            of this writing, and the District may either acquire the public
            infrastructure from the Developer or construct it, or even partly
            acquire it and partly construct it.

            The District intends to issue approximately $3,740,000 in par
            amount of Special Assessment Revenue Bonds, Series 2018
            (Assessment Area 2B) (the “Assessment Area 2B Bonds”) to fund
            approximately $3,189,563.20 in the Capital Improvement Program.
            The District also intends to issue approximately $12,025,000 in par
            amount of Special Assessment Revenue Bonds, Series 2018
            (Assessment Area 3A) (the “Assessment Area 3A Bonds”) to fund
            approximately $8,872,383.67 in the Capital Improvement Program.
            Additionally, the District intends to issue approximately $3,220,000
            in par amount of Special Assessment Revenue Bonds, Series 2018
            (Assessment Area 4) (the “Assessment Area 4 Bonds”, and
            cumulatively with Assessment Area 2B Bonds and Assessment
            Area 3A Bonds, the “Bonds”)) to fund approximately $2,726,385.39
            in the Capital Improvement Program. Additional improvements will
            be contributed to the District at no cost to the District under a
            Completion Agreement that will be entered into by the Developer
            and the District.

      4.2   Types of Bonds Proposed

            The proposed financing plan for Phase 2B within the District
            provides for the issuance of the Assessment Area 2B Bonds in the

                                             5
                                      D-35

approximate principal amount of $3,740,000 to finance costs
estimated at $3,189,563.20. The Assessment Area 2B Bonds are
projected to be structured to be amortized in 30 annual installments
following an approximately 11-month capitalized interest period.
Interest payments on the Assessment Area 2B Bonds would be
made every May 1 and November 1, and principal payments on the
Assessment Area 2B Bonds would be made every November 1.

In order to finance the improvement costs for Phase 2B, the District
would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the
total amount of $3,740,000. The difference is comprised of debt
service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
of funding for the Assessment Area 2B Bonds are presented in
Table 3 in the Appendix.

The proposed financing plan for Phase 3A within the District
provides for the issuance of the Assessment Area 3A Bonds in the
approximate principal amount of $12,025,000 to finance costs
estimated at $8,872,383.67. The Assessment Area 3A Bonds are
projected to be structured to be amortized in 30 annual installments
following an approximately 36-month capitalized interest period.
Interest payments on the Assessment Area 3A Bonds would be
made every May 1 and November 1, and principal payments on the
Assessment Area 3A Bonds would be made every November 1.

In order to finance the improvement costs Phase 3A, the District
would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the
total amount of $12,025,000. The difference is comprised of debt
service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
of funding for the Assessment Area 3A Bonds are presented in
Table 3 in the Appendix.

The proposed financing plan for Phase 4 within the District provides
for the issuance of the Assessment Area 4 Bonds in the
approximate principal amount of $3,220,000 to finance costs
estimated at $2,726,385.39. The Assessment Area 4 Bonds are
projected to be structured to be amortized in 30 annual installments
following an approximately 11-month capitalized interest period.
Interest payments on the Assessment Area 4 Bonds would be
made every May 1 and November 1, and principal payments on the
Assessment Area 4 Bonds would be made every November 1.

In order to finance the improvement costs Phase 4, the District
would need to borrow more funds and incur indebtedness in the

                                6
                         D-36

            total amount of $3,220,000. The difference is comprised of debt
            service reserve, capitalized interest, and costs of issuance,
            including the underwriter's discount. Preliminary sources and uses
            of funding for the Assessment Area 4 Bonds are presented in Table
            3 in the Appendix.


5.0   Assessment Methodology


      5.1   Overview

            The issuance of the Bonds provides the District with a portion of the
            funds necessary to construct/acquire the Capital Improvement
            Program outlined in Section 3.2 and described in more detail by the
            District Engineer in the Engineer's Report. These improvements
            yield special and general benefits, with special benefits accruing to
            properties within the boundaries of Phase III and general benefits
            accruing to areas outside of Phase III and outside the District and
            being only incidental in nature. The debt incurred in financing the
            portion of the public infrastructure included in the Capital
            Improvement Program will be paid off by assessing properties that
            derive special and peculiar benefits from the Capital Improvement
            Program. All properties that receive special benefits from the
            Capital Improvement Program will be assessed for their fair share
            of the debt issued in order to finance the Capital Improvement
            Program.

      5.2   Benefit Allocation

            The current development plan envisions the development of a total
            of 1,462 residential units, with 243 residential units for Phase 2B,
            1,053 residential units for Phase 3A, and 166 residential units for
            Phase 4, although unit numbers and land use types may change
            throughout the development period.

            As described in the Engineer’s Report, the Capital Improvement
            Program is comprised of the Series 2018 Master Project, which
            provides the master infrastructure for all three phases within the
            Phase III, and the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project, which
            provides the neighborhood infrastructure for Phase 2B only. The
            costs of the Series 2018 Master Project have been allocated by the
            District Engineer in accordance with each phase’s respective
            projected use and proportionate share of the Series 2018 Master
            Project improvements. As the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
            Project includes internal improvements specific to Phase 2B only,

                                            7
                                     D-37

the costs of the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project have been
allocated by the District Engineer to the Phase 2B only.

The public infrastructure included in the Series 2018 Master Project
portion of the Capital Improvement Program will comprise an
interrelated system of improvements within respectively
Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
4, which means that all of the improvements will serve respectively
Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
4, and such public improvements will be interrelated such that they
will reinforce each other and their combined benefits will be greater
than the sum of their individual benefits. All of the land uses within
Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
4 will benefit from each infrastructure improvement category, as
respectively the Series 2018 Master Project portion of the Capital
Improvement Program provides basic infrastructure to all lands
within Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and
Assessment Area 4 and benefits all lands within respectively
Assessment Area 2B, Assessment Area 3A, and Assessment Area
4 as an integrated system of improvements.

Similarly, the public infrastructure included in the Series 2018
Neighborhood 2B Project will comprise an interrelated system of
improvements within Assessment Area 2B, which means that all of
the improvements will serve Assessment Area 2B and such public
improvements will be interrelated such that they will reinforce each
other and their combined benefits will be greater than the sum of
their individual benefits. All of the land uses within Assessment
Area 2B will benefit from each infrastructure improvement category,
as respectively the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B Project portion of
the Capital Improvement Program provides basic infrastructure to
all lands within Assessment Area 2B and benefits all lands within
Assessment Area 2B as an integrated system of improvements.

As stated previously, the Capital Improvement Program has a
logical connection to the special and peculiar benefits received by
the land within Phase 2B, Phase 3A, and Phase 4, as without such
improvements, the development of the properties within Phase 2B,
Phase 3A, and Phase 4 would not be possible. Based upon the
connection between the improvements and the special and peculiar
benefits to the land within Phase 2B, Phase 3A, and Phase 4, the
District can assign or allocate a portion of the District's debt through
the imposition of non-ad valorem assessments, to the land
receiving such special and peculiar benefits. Even though these
special and peculiar benefits are real and ascertainable, the precise
amount of the benefit cannot yet be calculated with mathematical

                                 8
                          D-38

certainty. However, such benefit is more valuable than the cost of,
or the actual non-ad valorem assessment amount levied on that
parcel.

Phase 2B is projected to be developed with a total of 243
residential units and the benefit associated with the Phase 2B
portion of the Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital
Improvement Program as well as the Series 2018 Neighborhood 2B
Project component of the Capital Improvement Program is
proposed to be allocated to units within Phase 2B in proportion to
the density of development and intensity of use of infrastructure as
measured by a standard unit called an Equivalent Residential Unit
("ERU"). Table 4 in the Appendix illustrates the ERU weights that
are proposed to be assigned to the land uses contemplated to be
developed within Phase 2B based on the densities of development
and the intensities of use of infrastructure, the total ERU counts for
each land use category, for each phase and the share of the benefit
received by each land use within each phase.

Phase 3A is projected to be developed with a total of 1,053
residential units and the benefit associated with the Phase 3A
portion of the Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital
Improvement Program is proposed to be allocated to units within
Phase 3A in proportion to the density of development and intensity
of use of infrastructure as measured by the ERU. Table 4 in the
Appendix illustrates the ERU weights that are proposed to be
assigned to the land uses contemplated to be developed within
Phase 3A based on the densities of development and the
intensities of use of infrastructure, the total ERU counts for each
land use category, for each phase and the share of the benefit
received by each land use within each phase.

Phase 4 is projected to be developed with a total of 134 residential
units and the benefit associated with the Phase 4 portion of the
Series 2018 Master Project component of the Capital Improvement
Program is proposed to be allocated to units within Phase 4 in
proportion to the density of development and intensity of use of
infrastructure as measured by the ERU. Table 4 in the Appendix
illustrates the ERU weights that are proposed to be assigned to the
land uses contemplated to be developed within Phase 4 based on
the densities of development and the intensities of use of
infrastructure, the total ERU counts for each land use category, for
each phase and the share of the benefit received by each land use
within each phase.



                                9
                         D-39

      The rationale behind different ERU weights is supported by the fact
      that generally and on average smaller units will use and benefit
      from the District's infrastructure improvements less than larger
      units, as for instance, generally and on average smaller units
      produce less storm water runoff, may produce fewer vehicular trips,
      and may need less water/sewer capacity than larger units.
      Additionally, the value of the larger units is likely to appreciate by
      more in terms of dollars than that of the smaller units as a result of
      the implementation of the infrastructure improvements. As the
      exact amount of the benefit and appreciation is not possible to be
      calculated at this time, the use of ERU measures serves as a
      reasonable approximation of the relative amount of benefit received
      from the District's Capital Improvement Program.

      In order to facilitate the marketing of the residential units within
      Phase III, the Developer requested that the District limit the amount
      of annual assessments for debt service on the 2018 Bonds to
      certain predetermined levels. Table 5 presents the allocation of the
      Capital Improvement Program costs in each assessment area
      based on the ERU benefit allocation factors present in Table 4 in
      the Appendix. Further, Table 5 illustrates the approximate costs of
      the Capital Improvement Program for each assessment area that
      are projected to be financed with bonds, and the approximate costs
      of the Capital Improvement Program to be contributed by the
      Developer. In order to accomplish that goal, the Developer will
      contribute infrastructure improvements valued at $1,917,306.64 for
      Phase 2B, $5,672,307.77 for Phase 3A, and $414,053.33 for Phase
      4 as indicated in Table 5. Table 6 in the Appendix presents the
      apportionment of the assessment associated with the Bonds (the
      “Bond Assessment”) in accordance with the ERU benefit allocation
      method presented in Table 4 in the Appendix as modified by the
      effects of Developer’s contributions of infrastructure improvements.
      Table 6 in the Appendix also presents the annual levels of the
      projected annual debt service assessments per unit.

5.3   Assigning Bond Assessment

      In Phase 2B, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 2B Bonds (the “Assessment Area 2B Bond
      Assessment”) and will be levied initially on approximately 85.8 +/-
      gross acres within the Assessment Area 2B on an equal pro-rata
      gross acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $3,740,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 85.8 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $43,589.74 per acre.



                                      10
                               D-40

      In Phase 3A, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 3A Bonds (the “Assessment Area 3A Bond
      Assessment”) and will be levied initially on approximately 400.1 +/-
      gross acres within the Assessment Area 3A on an equal pro-rata
      gross acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $12,025,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 400.1 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $30,054.99 per acre.

      In Phase 4, the assessment associated with repayment of the
      Assessment Area 4 Bonds (the “Assessment Area 4 Bond
      Assessment”) and will be levied initially on approximately 74.1 +/-
      gross acres within the Assessment Area 4 on an equal pro-rata
      gross acre basis and thus the total bonded debt in the amount of
      $3,220,000 will be preliminarily levied on approximately 74.1 +/-
      gross acres at a rate of $43,454.79 per acre.

      As parcels are platted, the Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment,
      Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment, and Assessment Area 4
      Bond Assessment (cumulatively, the “Bond Assessment”) will be
      allocated to such parcels that are platted based on the planned use
      for that parcel as reflected in Table 6 in the Appendix.

      Further, to the extent that any parcel that has not been platted, is
      sold to another developer or builder, the Bond Assessment will be
      assigned to such parcel at the time of the sale based upon the
      development rights associated with such parcel that are transferred
      from seller to buyer. The District shall provide an estoppel or
      similar document to the buyer evidencing the amount of Bond
      Assessment transferred at sale.

5.4   Lienability Test: Special and Peculiar Benefit to the Property

      As first discussed in Section 1.3, Special Benefits and General
      Benefits, the Capital Improvement Program creates special and
      peculiar benefits to properties within Phase III. The Capital
      Improvement Program benefits assessable properties within Phase
      III and accrues to all such assessable properties on an ERU basis
      as modified in Table 4 in the Appendix.

      The Capital Improvement Program undertaken by Phase III can be
      shown to be creating special and peculiar benefits to the property
      within Phase III. The special and peculiar benefits resulting from
      each improvement are:
             a.    added use of the property;
             b.    added enjoyment of the property;
             c.    decreased insurance premiums; and

                                      11
                               D-41

            d.     increased marketability and value of the property.

      The Capital Improvement Program makes the land in Phase 2B,
      3A, and 4 developable and saleable and provides special and
      peculiar benefits which are greater than the benefits of any single
      category of improvements. These special and peculiar benefits are
      real and ascertainable, but not yet capable of being calculated and
      assessed in terms of numerical value; however, such benefits are
      more valuable than either the cost of, or the actual assessment
      levied for, the improvement or debt allocated to the parcel of land.

5.5   Lienability Test: Reasonable and Fair Apportionment of the
      Duty to Pay

      A reasonable estimate of the proportion of special and peculiar
      benefits received from the Capital Improvement Program is
      delineated in Table 3 (expressed as ERU factors) in the Appendix.

      The apportionment of the assessments is fair and reasonable
      because it was conducted on the basis of consistent application of
      the methodology described in Section 5.2 across all assessable
      property within the District according to reasonable estimates of the
      special and peculiar benefits derived from the Capital Improvement
      Program by different land uses.

      Accordingly, no acre or parcel of property within the District will be
      liened for the payment of any non-ad valorem special assessment
      more than the determined special benefit peculiar to that property.

5.6   True-Up Mechanism

      The Assessment Methodology described herein is based on
      conceptual information obtained from the Developer prior to
      construction. As development occurs it is possible that the number
      of units and types of units may change within each assessment
      area. The mechanism for maintaining the methodology over the
      changes is referred to as true-up.

      This mechanism is to be utilized to ensure that the Assessment
      Area 2B Bond Assessment on a per unit basis never exceeds the
      assessment as contemplated in the adopted assessment
      methodology. The Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment per unit
      for the different unit types are listed in Table 6 and may change
      based on the final bond sizing. The Assessment Area 3A Bond
      Assessment per unit for the different unit types are listed in Table 6
      and may change based on the final bond sizing. The Assessment

                                      12
                               D-42

Area 4 Bond Assessment per unit for the different unit types are
listed in Table 6 and may change based on the final bond sizing. If
such changes occur, the Methodology is applied to the land based
on the number of and type of units of particular land uses within
each and every parcel as signified by the number of units and unit
types within each assessment area.

As the land in the District is platted, the Bond Assessment is
assigned to platted parcels based on the figures in Table 6 in the
Appendix. If as a result of platting and apportionment of the
Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment to the platted parcels, the
Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment
Area 2B that remains unplatted remains equal to the levels in Table
6, then no true-up adjustment will be necessary. Similarly, if as a
result of platting and apportionment of the Assessment Area 3A
Bond Assessment to the platted parcels, the Assessment Area 3A
Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment Area 3A that remains
unplatted remains equal to the levels in Table 6, then no true-up
adjustment will be necessary. Additionally, if as a result of platting
and apportionment of the Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment to
the platted parcels, the Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment per
unit in Assessment Area 4 that remains unplatted remains equal to
the levels in Table 6, then no true-up adjustment will be necessary.

If as a result of platting and apportionment of the Assessment Area
2B Bond Assessment to the platted parcels the Assessment Area
2B Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment Area 2B that remains
unplatted equals less than the levels in Table 6 (either as a result of
a larger number of units, different units or both), then the per unit
Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment for all parcels within
Assessment Area 2B will be lowered if that state persists at the
conclusion of platting of all land within the District. If as a result of
platting and apportionment of the Assessment Area 3A Bond
Assessment to the platted parcels for land in Assessment Area 3A,
the Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment
Area 3A that remains unplatted equals less than the levels in Table
6 (either as a result of a larger number of units, different units or
both), then the per unit Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment for
all parcels within Assessment Area 3A will be lowered if that state
persists at the conclusion of platting of all land within the District. If
as a result of platting and apportionment of the Assessment Area 4
Bond Assessment to the platted parcels in Assessment Area 4, the
Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment Area
4 that remains unplatted equals less than the levels in Table 6
(either as a result of a larger number of units, different units or
both), then the per unit Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment for

                                  13
                           D-43

all parcels within Assessment Area 4 will be lowered if that state
persists at the conclusion of platting of all land within the District.

If, in contrast, as a result of platting and apportionment of the
Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment to the platted parcels in
Assessment Area 2B, the Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment
per unit in Assessment Area 2B that remains unplatted equals more
than the levels in Table 6 (either as a result of a smaller number of
units, different units or both), taking into account any future
development plans for the unplatted lands – in the District’s sole
discretion and to the extent such future development plans are
feasible, consistent with existing entitlements and governmental
requirements, and reasonably expected to be implemented, then
the difference in Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment plus
accrued interest will be collected from the owner of the property
which platting caused the increase of assessment per unit to occur,
in accordance with the assessment resolution and/or a true-up
agreement to be entered into between the District and the
Developer, which will be binding on assignees. If, in contrast, as a
result of platting and apportionment of the Assessment Area 3A
Bond Assessment to the platted parcels in Assessment Area 3A,
the Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment
Area 3A that remains unplatted equals more than the levels in
Table 6 (either as a result of a smaller number of units, different
units or both), taking into account any future development plans for
the unplatted lands – in the District’s sole discretion and to the
extent such future development plans are feasible, consistent with
existing entitlements and governmental requirements, and
reasonably expected to be implemented, then the difference in
Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment plus accrued interest will
be collected from the owner of the property which platting caused
the increase of assessment per unit to occur, in accordance with
the assessment resolution and/or a true-up agreement to be
entered into between the District and the Developer, which will be
binding on assignees. If, in contrast, as a result of platting and
apportionment of the Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment to the
platted parcels in Assessment Area 4, the Assessment Area 4
Bond Assessment per unit in Assessment Area 4 that remains
unplatted equals more than the levels in Table 6 (either as a result
of a smaller number of units, different units or both), taking into
account any future development plans for the unplatted lands – in
the District’s sole discretion and to the extent such future
development plans are feasible, consistent with existing
entitlements and governmental requirements, and reasonably
expected to be implemented, then the difference in Assessment
Area 4 Bond Assessment plus accrued interest will be collected

                                 14
                          D-44

from the owner of the property which platting caused the increase
of assessment per unit to occur, in accordance with the
assessment resolution and/or a true-up agreement to be entered
into between the District and the Developer, which will be binding
on assignees.

The owner(s) of the property within Assessment Area 2B will be
required to immediately remit to the Trustee for redemption a true-
up payment equal to the difference between the actual Assessment
Area 2B Bond Assessment per unit and the levels in Table 6,
multiplied by the actual number of units in Assessment Area 2B
plus accrued interest to the next succeeding interest payment date
on the Assessment Area 2B Bonds, unless such interest payment
date occurs within 45 days of such true-up payment, in which case
the accrued interest shall be paid to the following interest payment
date (or such other time as set forth in the supplemental indenture
for the applicable series of Assessment Area 2B Bonds secured by
the Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment). The owner(s) of the
property within Assessment Area 3A will be required to immediately
remit to the Trustee for redemption a true-up payment equal to the
difference between the actual Assessment Area 3A Bond
Assessment per unit and the levels in Table 6, multiplied by the
actual number of units in Assessment Area 3A plus accrued
interest to the next succeeding interest payment date on the
Assessment Area 3A Bonds, unless such interest payment date
occurs within 45 days of such true-up payment, in which case the
accrued interest shall be paid to the following interest payment date
(or such other time as set forth in the supplemental indenture for
the applicable series of Assessment Area 3A Bonds secured by the
Assessment Area 3A Bond Assessment). The owner(s) of the
property within Assessment Area 4 will be required to immediately
remit to the Trustee for redemption a true-up payment equal to the
difference between the actual Assessment Area 4 Bond
Assessment per unit and the levels in Table 6, multiplied by the
actual number of units in Assessment Area 4 plus accrued interest
to the next succeeding interest payment date on the Assessment
Area 4 Bonds, unless such interest payment date occurs within 45
days of such true-up payment, in which case the accrued interest
shall be paid to the following interest payment date (or such other
time as set forth in the supplemental indenture for the applicable
series of Assessment Area 4 Bonds secured by the Assessment
Area 4 Bond Assessment).

In addition to platting of property within Assessment Area 2B,
Assessment Area 3A and/or Assessment Area 4, any planned sale
of an unplatted parcel to another builder or developer will cause the

                                15
                         D-45

            District to initiate a true-up test as described above to test whether
            the amount of the Assessment Area 2B Bond Assessment per unit
            that remains unplatted within Assessment Area 2B remains equal
            to the levels in Table 6, the amount of the Assessment Area 3A
            Bond Assessment per unit that remains unplatted within
            Assessment Area 3A remains equal to the levels in Table 6, and/or
            the amount of the Assessment Area 4 Bond Assessment per unit
            that remains unplatted within Assessment Area 4 remains equal to
            the levels in Table 6. The test will be based upon the development
            rights as signified by the number of units associated with such
            parcel that are transferred from seller to buyer. The District shall
            provide an estoppel or similar document to the buyer evidencing
            the amount of Bond Assessment transferred at sale.

            Note that, in the event that the Capital Improvement Plan is not
            completed, certain contributions are not made, multiple bond
            issuances are contemplated and not all are issued, or under certain
            other circumstances, the District may be required to reallocate the
            Bond Assessment.

      5.7   Final Assessment Roll

            The final assessment roll is included in Exhibit A.


6.0   Additional Stipulations


      6.1   Overview

            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC was retained by the District to
            prepare a methodology to fairly allocate the special assessments
            related to the District’s Capital Improvement Program. Certain
            financing, development and engineering data was provided by
            members of District Staff and/or the Developer. The allocation
            Methodology described herein was based on information provided
            by those professionals. Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC makes
            no representations regarding said information transactions beyond
            restatement of the factual information necessary for compilation of
            this report. For additional information on the Bond structure and
            related items, please refer to the Offering Statement associated
            with this transaction.

            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC does not represent the
            District as a Municipal Advisor or Securities Broker nor is
            Wrathell, Hunt and Associates, LLC registered to provide such

                                             16
                                      D-46

                services as described in Section 15B of the Securities and
                Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. Similarly, Wrathell, Hunt
                and Associates, LLC does not provide the District with
                financial advisory services or offer investment advice in any
                form.
